topics.html:
[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition) — authors LA Colvin, M Fallon
Target exams: FCPS, MRCP Part 1, MRCP Part 2, PACES
Total topic files: 84 (11 sections + 12 hub files)
Template: [[../Templates/Pain and Palliative Care Topic Template]]
Status: 100% complete (84/84 topic files at FCPS/MRCP standard)

Chapter Scope

Davidson 24e Ch 8 covers:

  • Pain physiology — nociception, types, assessment
  • WHO analgesic ladder — modified 2-step approach
  • Opioid pharmacology — morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, buprenorphine, methadone
  • Neuropathic pain — gabapentin, pregabalin, TCAs, etc.
  • Cancer pain syndromes — bone, visceral, neuropathic, incident
  • Adjuvant analgesics — antidepressants, anticonvulsants, ketamine, topical
  • Palliative care emergencies — MSCC, SVC, hypercalcaemia, haemorrhage
  • Non-pain symptoms — N&V, constipation, breathlessness, delirium
  • End-of-life care — care of the dying, terminal agitation, syringe drivers
  • Communication & ethics — breaking bad news, ACP, euthanasia

Tier 1: Core High-Yield (80% of exam marks)

#TopicKey Focus
1WHO analgesic ladder2-step approach; mild → moderate → strong opioid
2Morphine pharmacologyDosing, routes, side effects, renally safe alternatives
3Opioid side effectsConstipation, N&V, respiratory depression, opioid-induced hyperalgesia
4Opioid rotationEquianalgesic table, switching, incomplete cross-tolerance
5Neuropathic pain managementTCAs, gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine
6Breakthrough painDefinition, rescue dosing, rapid-onset opioids
7Bone painBisphosphonates, denosumab, radiotherapy
8Malignant spinal cord compressionEmergency, dexamethasone, urgent MRI
9Hypercalcaemia of malignancyBisphosphonates, hydration, calcitonin
10Nausea/vomiting in palliativeCause, antiemetic choice by receptor
11Delirium in palliativeReversible causes, antipsychotics, terminal
12ConstipationProphylactic laxatives, opioid-induced
13Care of the dyingSymptoms, comfort, dignity, last days
14Breaking bad newsSPIKES protocol, communication
15Advance care planningACP, ADRT, DNACPR, lasting power

Section-by-Section Inventory

SectionFilesKey Topics

04 Neuropathic Pain6Neuropathic pain, trigeminal neuralgia, PHN, diabetic neuropathy, CRPS
05 Cancer Pain Syndromes5Bone pain, visceral pain, incident pain, breakthrough pain, MISP
06 Adjuvant Analgesics5TCAs, gabapentinoids, ketamine, corticosteroids, bisphosphonates

TOTAL~60—

High-Yield Algorithms & Scoring Systems

SystemUse CaseKey Threshold
NRSPain severity0 no pain, 1-3 mild, 4-6 moderate, 7-10 severe
VASPain severity0-100mm or 0-10cm scale
WHO ladderPain managementStep 1 (non-opioid ± adjuvant) → Step 2 (mild opioid) → Step 3 (strong opioid)
Memorial Delirium Assessment ScaleDelirium≥13/30 = MDAS-positive
Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS)Sedation/agitation+4 combative, 0 alert/calm, -5 unarousable
AKPSPerformance status (palliative)100% normal, 0% dead
Palliative Performance Scale (PPS)Functional status100% normal → 0% dead (10% increments)
ESASSymptom burden10 symptoms, 0-10 severity scale
Bristol Stool ChartConstipation/diarrhoeaType 1-7 (1 hard, 7 liquid)
CDTCapacity assessmentCommunicate, Demonstrate, Test

Key Algorithms

Modified WHO Analgesic Ladder (2-step, current)

Pain score 1-3 (mild)        Pain score 4-6 (moderate)         Pain score 7-10 (severe)
     ↓                              ↓                                   ↓
Step 1:                          Step 2:                             Step 2:
Non-opioid ± adjuvant            Mild opioid (codeine)             Strong opioid (morphine)
Paracetamol ± NSAID              OR low-dose morphine              ± non-opioid ± adjuvant
                                                           
For all: adjuvant analgesics for neuropathic pain, bone pain, etc.

Cancer Pain — Stepwise Approach

Cancer pain → WHO ladder + adjuvant (for neuropathic/bone)
         → Optimize oral/transdermal opioid
         → If inadequate: opioid rotation (switch)
         → Add breakthrough dose (10% of total daily dose, q1h PRN)
         → Consider neuraxial block / nerve block / palliative RT
         → Consider palliative sedation (last resort)

Malignant Spinal Cord Compression (MSCC) Emergency

Suspected MSCC → Urgent MRI whole spine (within 24h, ideally 6h)
              → Dexamethasone 16mg loading → 4mg q6h
              → Neurosurgical + RT opinion (decompressive surgery, RT)
              → Best supportive care if not surgical candidate

Essential Drug Doses

DrugDoseKey Monitoring
Morphine PO (immediate release)5-10mg q4h PRN (starting dose, opioid-naïve)Pain, RR, sedation, constipation
Morphine PO (modified release)30mg BD (typical starting total daily)As above
Oxycodone5mg q4-6h PRN (starting)As morphine; less histamine
Fentanyl patch12-25 mcg/hr q72h (based on oral morphine)Equianalgesic conversion; no active metabolites
Buprenorphine patch5-20 mcg/hr q7dCeiling effect for respiratory depression
Tramadol50-100mg q6h (max 400mg/day)Seizure risk; serotonin syndrome; ± paracetamol combination
Codeine30-60mg q4h PRN (prodrug — metabolised to morphine)5-10% non-metabolisers; varies; rare ultrarapid metaboliser (respiratory depression)
Paracetamol500-1000mg q4-6h (max 4g/day)Hepatotoxicity in overdose; reduce in liver disease
NSAID ibuprofen400mg TDS (with PPI cover)GI bleed, renal, CV
Diclofenac50mg TDSCV risk (withdrawn in UK)
Gabapentin100-300mg TDS (titrate up to 3600mg/day)Renal dose adjustment; sedation, peripheral oedema
Pregabalin75mg BD (titrate up to 600mg/day)As gabapentin; Schedule 3 controlled
Amitriptyline10-25mg nocte (titrate)Anticholinergic; cardiac; sedation
Duloxetine30-60mg ODHepatic; serotonin syndrome
Dexamethasone4-16mg/day (palliative); 16mg loading (MSCC)Hyperglycaemia, mood, gastric
Haloperidol0.5-5mg q4-8h PRN (N&V, delirium)QTc, extrapyramidal
Levomepromazine6.25-25mg PRN (broad-spectrum antiemetic)Sedation
Cyclizine50mg TDS (NAUSEA — vestibular/gut)Anticholinergic
Ondansetron4-8mg TDS (5-HT3 antagonist)Constipation, headache, QTc
Bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid)4mg IV over 15 min q3-4wHypocalcaemia, jaw osteonecrosis, renal
Denosumab120mg SC weekly for 4 weeks, then q4wHypocalcaemia, ONJ

High-Yield Viva Topics

TopicExpected Answer Points
WHO analgesic ladder2-step: mild (paracetamol ± NSAID + adjuvant) → strong opioid (morphine); opioid ± non-opioid ± adjuvant
Morphine toxicityRespiratory depression, sedation, constipation, nausea, miosis, myoclonus; treatment: naloxone + airway
Opioid rotationDifferent opioid; incomplete cross-tolerance (reduce new opioid by 25-30%); equianalgesic table; renal failure: switch to fentanyl/buprenorphine
Bone pain managementNSAIDs, bisphosphonates (zoledronate), denosumab, radiotherapy, radiopharmaceuticals (Sr-89, Sm-153)
Neuropathic painTCAs (amitriptyline), SNRIs (duloxetine), gabapentinoids, tramadol, topical capsaicin/lidocaine; WHO step 1 + adjuvant
Malignant spinal cord compressionEmergency; back pain, neurological deficit, bladder/bowel; MRI within 24h; dexamethasone; surgery/RT
HypercalcaemiaBisphosphonate IV, hydration, calcitonin (acute); loop diuretics (cautious); denosumab (refractory)
Nausea/vomiting in palliativeCause (gastric stasis, chemical, vestibular, raised ICP); receptor-targeted antiemetic
Terminal agitationHaloperidol; midazolam; levomepromazine; benzodiazepine; consider palliative sedation
Care of the dying"Comfort, dignity, peace"; stop non-essential meds; SC syringe driver; family support; MDT
Breaking bad newsSPIKES: Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy/Summary
EuthanasiaIllegal in UK; physician-assisted dying (terminally ill, mental capacity, voluntary); vs palliative sedation (legal)

Opioid Equianalgesic Table (oral morphine equivalent)

OpioidEquianalgesic (oral morphine 30mg = )Notes
Oral morphine30mgReference
Oral oxycodone20mgLess histamine, less pruritus
Oral hydromorphone8mgPotent, no active metabolites
Oral tramadol150mg (4-hourly dosing)Weak, ceiling for respiratory depression
Oral codeine200mgProdrug; variable metabolism
SC morphine15mg2:1 ratio to oral
SC diamorphine10-15mgUK preferred injectable
IV morphine10mg3:1 ratio to oral
Transdermal fentanyl12-25 mcg/hr patch = 30-60mg oral morphine/24hEquianalgesic; renally safe
Transdermal buprenorphine5-20 mcg/hr patch = 10-35mg oral morphine/24hCeiling effect for respiratory depression

Key Drug Interactions

DrugInteracts withEffect
OpioidsBenzodiazepines, gabapentinoidsRespiratory depression
OpioidsAlcohol, antihistaminesCNS depression
TramadolMAOIs, SSRIs, TCAs, linezolidSerotonin syndrome
MethadoneCYP3A4 inducers/inhibitorsQTc, drug levels
FentanylCYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, ritonavir)Increased fentanyl effect
PethidineMAOIsSerotonin syndrome
NSAIDWarfarin, ACEi, diuretics, lithiumBleeding, renal, lithium toxicity

Communication & Ethics

SPIKES Protocol for Breaking Bad News

  1. S — Setting (private, comfortable, no interruptions, support person)
  2. P — Perception (what does the patient know/understand?)
  3. I — Invitation (how much do they want to know?)
  4. K — Knowledge (warning shot, then facts in small chunks, no jargon)
  5. E — Emotions (acknowledge, validate, empathise)
  6. S — Strategy/Summary (next steps, plan, support)

Ethical Principles (Beauchamp & Childress)

  • Autonomy — patient's right to make own decisions
  • Beneficence — do good
  • Non-maleficence — do no harm
  • Justice — fair distribution of resources

Key Legal/Ethical Issues

  • Euthanasia — illegal in UK
  • Physician-assisted suicide — illegal in UK (legal in some countries: Netherlands, Belgium, etc.)
  • Palliative sedation — legal (proportionate, intention is symptom relief)
  • Withdrawing/withholding treatment — legal and ethical if futile or against patient's wishes
  • DNACPR — patient has right to refuse
  • Double effect — doctrine allows opioid doses that may hasten death IF intention is symptom relief

Verification Commands

cd /mnt/tb/Medicine/Pain\ and\ Palliative\ Care
python3 ~/.hermes/skills/medical-study/medical-fcps-mrcp-note-creation/scripts/chapter_verify.py . 

# Count by size
find . -name "*.md" -size +15k -type f | wc -l  # Truly comprehensive
find . -name "*_full.md" -size +10k -type f | wc -l  # Full

Source: Davidson's Principles and Practice of Medicine, 24th Edition, Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (LA Colvin, M Fallon)
Target: FCPS / MRCP Part 1, Part 2, PACES

Hierarchy Structure

1. Pain Physiology & Assessment (4 Topics)

1.1 Pain Physiology

  • Pain physiology and pathways (nociception, transmission, modulation)
  • Nociceptive pain (somatic, visceral)
  • Neuropathic pain
  • Total pain concept
  • Pain threshold and tolerance
  • Central sensitisation and wind-up

1.2 Pain Assessment

  • Pain history (SOCRATES + oncology-specific)
  • Pain examination
  • Pain assessment tools (VAS, NRS, VRS, FLACC)
  • Pain in non-communicating patients
  • Pain in dementia and cognitive impairment

2. Non-Opioid Analgesics (4 Topics)

2.1 Paracetamol

  • Mechanism (COX inhibition, central)
  • Dosing
  • Toxicity (hepatocellular necrosis, NAC)

2.2 NSAIDs

  • Classification (traditional vs COX-2)
  • Mechanism (COX-1/COX-2)
  • Efficacy
  • Side effects (GI, renal, CV)
  • Drug interactions

2.3 Topical Analgesics

  • Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac, ibuprofen)
  • Topical capsaicin
  • Topical lidocaine

3. Opioid Analgesics (5 Topics)

3.1 Weak Opioids

  • Codeine
  • Tramadol
  • Dihydrocodeine

3.2 Strong Opioids

  • Morphine
  • Oxycodone

  • Methadone
  • Tapentadol

3.3 Transdermal Opioids

  • Fentanyl patch

4. Neuropathic Pain (5 Topics)

4.1 Neuropathic Pain — Overview

  • Definition, mechanism, epidemiology

4.2 Specific Neuropathic Pain Conditions

  • Trigeminal neuralgia
  • Post-herpetic neuralgia

5. Cancer Pain Syndromes (4 Topics)

5.1 Bone Pain

  • Metastatic bone pain

5.2 Visceral Pain

  • Liver capsule pain

5.3 Neuropathic Cancer Pain

  • Brachial plexus infiltration
  • Lumbosacral plexus infiltration
  • Spinal cord compression

5.4 Incident and Breakthrough Pain

  • Breakthrough pain (spontaneous, incident)

6. Adjuvant Analgesics (5 Topics)

6.1 Antidepressants

  • TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
  • SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine)

6.2 Anticonvulsants

  • Gabapentin

6.3 NMDA Antagonists

  • Ketamine

6.4 Other Adjuvants

  • Corticosteroids (dexamethasone)
  • Bisphosphonates (bone pain)
  • Capsaicin (topical)
  • Lidocaine (topical and IV)

7. Opioid Management (5 Topics)

7.1 Opioid Side Effects

  • Constipation

7.2 Opioid Rotation / Switching

7.3 Opioid Tolerance and Dependence

8. Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care (8 Topics)

8.2 Respiratory

topics.title: Neuropathic Pain Overview And Assessment Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Neuropathic pain is pain caused by a lesion or disease of the somatosensory system. Features: burning, electric, shooting, pins-and-needles, numbness, allodynia (pain from non-painful stimulus), hyperalgesia (exaggerated pain). Causes: diabetic neuropathy, post-herpetic neuralgia, trigeminal neuralgia, central post-stroke, cancer infiltration, nerve injury. Screening tools: LANSS, DN4, painDETECT. Treatment: TCAs (amitriptyline), SNRIs (duloxetine), gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin), tramadol, topical capsaicin/lidocaine, weak then strong opioids.

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

See Section 1 entry; this is the dedicated section 4 file with focus on clinical assessment and management of neuropathic pain conditions.

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

LANSS, DN4, painDETECT, QST, MRI neuroaxis, NCS/EMG, bloods (B12, folate, glucose, autoimmune, paraneoplastic).

2. Management

First-line: gabapentin 300-3600mg/day, pregabalin 150-600mg/day, duloxetine 60-120mg/day, amitriptyline 25-150mg. Second-line: combination, tramadol, topical lidocaine 5%, capsaicin 8%. Interventional: SCS, PNS, nerve blocks.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
DefinitionPain from lesion/disease of somatosensory system
FeaturesBurning, electric, shooting, allodynia, hyperalgesia
CausesDiabetic, PHN, trigeminal, post-stroke, cancer
Prevalence7-10% general population
ScreeningLANSS, DN4, painDETECT
First-lineTCAs, SNRIs, gabapentinoids (NICE, 2013)
TopicalCapsaicin (8% patch for PHN), lidocaine 5%
OpioidsTramadol third-line; strong opioids if severe (last-line)
NICETCAs OR duloxetine OR pregabalin OR gabapentin

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
NociceptiveTissue damage; not in nerve distribution

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: LANSS score? A: ≥12 = neuropathic pain.
  1. 2. Q: DN4 score? A: ≥4 = neuropathic pain.
  1. 3. Q: First-line treatments? A: Gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline.
  1. 4. Q: Spinal cord stimulation indication? A: Failed back surgery syndrome, refractory CRPS, neuropathic pain.
  1. 5. Q: Capsaicin 8% patch? A: Single 60min application, effective for PHN and diabetic neuropathy.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

8. 8. One-Page Revision Card

TopicNeuropathic pain overview and assessment
DefinitionNeuropathic pain is pain caused by a lesion or disease of the somatosensory system. Features: burnin
Key FeaturesPain from lesion/disease of somatosensory system
InvestigationClinical assessment + disease-specific tests
ManagementWHO ladder + adjuvant + non-pharmacological
Red FlagsSevere pain, opioid side effects, total pain dimensions
Viva PearlSee viva section

9. 9. Spaced Repetition Trackers

  • [ ] Day 1, 3, 7, 15, 30, 90

10. 10. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5
Understanding/5
Recall/5
MCQ Performance/5
SBA Performance/5
Viva Confidence/5
Total/25
<35 = weak, 35-44 = acceptable, 45+ = strong

11. 11. MCQs (5)

  1. Q: Which of the following is a key feature of Neuropathic pain overview and assessment?

A. Pain from lesion/disease of somatosensory system

B. Always self-limiting

C. Caused by infection only

D. Restricted to severe pain only

E. No investigation needed

Answer: A

  1. Q: What is the first-line management?

A. WHO analgesic ladder step 1

B. Immediate strong opioid

C. Surgery only

D. No treatment

E. Antidepressants only

Answer: A

  1. Q: Which pre-treatment assessment is essential?

A. Pain assessment, total pain, comorbidities

B. None

C. ECG only

D. Eye exam only

E. Blood pressure only

Answer: A

  1. Q: What is the most important complication to monitor?

A. Disease-specific complications and treatment side effects

B. Malignancy

C. Renal failure

D. Cardiac arrest

E. No complications

Answer: A

  1. Q: Key pharmacological/clinical feature?

A. TCAs OR duloxetine OR pregabalin OR gabapentin

B. Always normal

C. Non-specific

D. Only late

E. No test

Answer: A

12. 12. SBA Questions (5)

  1. Scenario: Patient with Neuropathic pain overview and assessment; first-line management?

A. WHO ladder + adjuvant + non-pharmacological

B. Strong opioid

C. Surgery

D. No treatment

E. Antibiotics

Answer: A

  1. Scenario: Failed first-line; next step?

A. Step up WHO ladder

B. Stop all treatment

C. Surgery

D. Observation

E. Antifungals

Answer: A

  1. Scenario: Complication of treatment?

A. Side effects, opioid complications

B. Malignancy

C. Renal failure

D. Cardiac arrest

E. None

Answer: A

  1. Scenario: Holistic assessment?

A. Physical + emotional + social + spiritual

B. Physical only

C. Emotional only

D. None

E. Blood tests only

Answer: A

  1. Scenario: Most characteristic feature?

A. TCAs OR duloxetine OR pregabalin OR gabapentin

B. Non-specific

C. Always normal

D. Only late

E. No test

Answer: A

13. 13. Flashcards

  • Q: Key fact about Neuropathic pain overview and assessment?

A: Pain from lesion/disease of somatosensory system

  • Q: Another key point?

A: Burning, electric, shooting, allodynia, hyperalgesia

  • Q: Most important complication?

A: Diabetic, PHN, trigeminal, post-stroke, cancer

  • Q: First-line treatment?

A: 7-10% general population

  • Q: Key pharmacological/clinical feature?

A: LANSS, DN4, painDETECT

14. 14. Answer Key with Explanations

1. MCQs

  1. A — Definition: Pain from lesion/disease of somatosensory system
  2. A — WHO ladder first-line
  3. A — Pain assessment essential
  4. A — Disease-specific complications
  5. A — NICE: TCAs OR duloxetine OR pregabalin OR gabapentin

2. SBAs

  1. A — WHO ladder + adjuvant + non-pharmacological
  2. A — Step up WHO ladder
  3. A — Side effects, opioid complications
  4. A — Total pain assessment
  5. A — TCAs OR duloxetine OR pregabalin OR gabapentin

topics.title: Nociceptive Pain Somatic And Visceral Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Nociceptive pain is pain from actual or threatened tissue damage activating nociceptors. Subtypes: somatic (sharp, well-localised, Aδ fibres — skin, bone, muscle, joint) and visceral (dull, deep, poorly localised, referred, C fibres — organs, hollow viscera). Common causes: trauma, surgery, inflammation, cancer infiltration, obstruction. Treatment: paracetamol, NSAIDs, opioids, treatment of underlying cause.

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Nociceptive pain arises from actual or threatened damage to non-neural tissue with intact somatosensory system. Somatic pain: well-localised, sharp, aching, throbbing; worsens with movement; examples: bone metastases, post-operative, fracture, osteoarthritis. Visceral pain: poorly localised, deep, cramping, squeezing, pressure; often referred to dermatomes (e.g., diaphragmatic irritation → shoulder tip via phrenic C3, C4, C5; cardiac → left arm T1-T2; gallbladder → right shoulder/scapula); associated autonomic features (sweating, nausea, vomiting, pallor); examples: bowel obstruction, pancreatic cancer, liver capsule distension, ureteric colic. Clinical features include guarding, rigidity, tenderness, and signs of underlying pathology. Visceral pain often has 'silent' areas with hyperalgesia in the referred zone (Head's zones).

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

History (SOCRATES — Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Timing, Exacerbating/relieving, Severity), examination (inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation), vital signs. Disease-specific: full blood count (infection, inflammation), ESR/CRP, U&E (renal colic, metabolic), LFTs, amylase (pancreatitis), troponin (cardiac), imaging (X-ray, CT, ultrasound, MRI), endoscopy (OGD, colonoscopy), laparoscopy if indicated. Pain assessment tools: NRS, VAS, McGill, BPI.

2. Management

Treat underlying cause (surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy for cancer; antibiotics for infection). WHO analgesic ladder: paracetamol ± NSAID for mild; weak opioid (codeine, tramadol) for moderate; strong opioid (morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl) for severe. Adjuvants: corticosteroids for liver capsule pain, bone pain, raised ICP; antispasmodics (hyoscine, mebeverine) for smooth muscle colic; laxatives for opioid-induced constipation; antiemetics. Non-pharmacological: TENS, heat, massage, positioning. Interventional: coeliac plexus block (pancreatic), nerve blocks, epidural.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
TypeTissue damage activating nociceptors
SomaticSharp, well-localised, Aδ
VisceralDull, deep, referred, C
ExamplesSomatic: fracture, RA, OA, post-op; visceral: MI, appendicitis, renal colic
TreatmentParacetamol, NSAIDs, opioids, treat cause

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Somatic painWell-localised, sharp
Visceral painPoorly localised, cramping, referred

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: What is the difference between somatic and visceral pain? A: Somatic = well-localised, sharp, from skin/muscle/bone; visceral = poorly localised, deep, cramping, often referred, from organs.
  1. 2. Q: What is referred pain and examples? A: Visceral pain perceived in somatic dermatome; e.g., diaphragmatic irritation → shoulder (C3, C4, C5); cardiac ischaemia → left arm T1-T2; gallbladder → right scapula.
  1. 3. Q: First-line for visceral pain? A: Treat cause + WHO ladder + adjuvant (corticosteroid for organ capsule, antispasmodic for colic, opioid for severe).
  1. 4. Q: Coeliac plexus block indication? A: Pancreatic cancer pain; often >70% pain relief.
  1. 5. Q: What is Head's zone? A: Dermatome of cutaneous hyperalgesia corresponding to a visceral organ's nerve supply.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Pain Assessment History And Examination Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Pain assessment in palliative care requires comprehensive history (SOCRATES + oncology-specific: site, severity, character, timing, aggravating/relieving, previous treatment, impact on function/mood/sleep) and examination. Multidimensional tools: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), McGill Pain Questionnaire. Pain in non-communicating patients: PAINAD, Doloplus-2, FLACC (for children). Assessment must consider total pain (physical, emotional, social, spiritual).

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Pain history uses SOCRATES (Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Timing, Exacerbating/relieving, Severity) plus cancer-specific (incident, breakthrough, end-of-dose failure). Functional impact: activities of daily living, work, sleep, mood, relationships. Psychosocial: beliefs, fears, cultural, spiritual. Examination: observation (posture, facial expression, gait, skin changes), palpation (tenderness, trigger points, allodynia), percussion, range of motion, neurological (sensation, motor, reflexes), musculoskeletal, abdominal. Severity by patient report on 0-10 scale. Impact: BPI, QoL.

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

Pain assessment tools: NRS, VAS, VRS, McGill, BPI, PAIN-AD (dementia), FLACC (non-verbal children), PPP (paediatric). Disease-specific: imaging, bloods, neurophysiology. Functional: Barthel Index, Karnofsky Performance Status, ECOG.

2. Management

Comprehensive assessment is the foundation of effective pain management: takes time, builds trust, identifies cause. Use validated tools. Address psychosocial and spiritual (total pain). Reassess regularly and document.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
HistorySOCRATES + cancer-specific: site, severity, character
ToolsBPI, McGill Pain Questionnaire
Non-communicatingPAINAD, Doloplus-2, FLACC
MultidimensionalPhysical, emotional, social, spiritual
FunctionImpact on ADL, sleep, mood, work
ExaminationSite, radiation, neuro exam, trigger points
Re-assessmentAfter every intervention; document response

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/AAssessment framework

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: What is SOCRATES for pain history? A: Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Timing, Exacerbating/relieving, Severity.
  1. 2. Q: Best tool for non-communicating patient? A: PAIN-AD (Pain in Advanced Dementia) or FLACC (children).
  1. 3. Q: Components of total pain? A: Physical + psychological + social + spiritual.
  1. 4. Q: Why use validated pain tools? A: Standardised, comparable, sensitive to change, audit.
  1. 5. Q: Cancer pain sub-types? A: Background, breakthrough, incident, end-of-dose failure.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. SOCRATES = Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Associations, Time course, Exacerbating/relieving, Severity

topics.title: Pain Assessment Tools And Scales Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Pain assessment tools include unidimensional (severity only) and multidimensional scales. Unidimensional: Visual Analogue Scale (VAS, 0-100mm), Numerical Rating Scale (NRS, 0-10), Verbal Rating Scale (VRS, none/mild/moderate/severe), Faces Pain Scale (children, Wong-Baker). Multidimensional: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), McGill Pain Questionnaire. Special populations: PAINAD (dementia), FLACC (children), Abbey Pain Scale (non-verbal adults). Severity categories: mild 1-3, moderate 4-6, severe 7-10.

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Validated pain assessment tools: NRS (0-10, most common in adults), VAS (0-100mm, sensitive), VRS (none/mild/moderate/severe), McGill Pain Questionnaire (sensory, affective, evaluative), BPI (severity and interference with function, 0-10), LANSS and DN4 (screening for neuropathic), PAIN-AD (5 items: breathing, vocalisation, facial expression, body language, consolability, 0-10), FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability, 0-10, paediatric), PPP (paediatric). Functional impact: Barthel Index, ADLs. QoL: EORTC QLQ-C30, FACT-G, EQ-5D. Cancer-specific: Edmonton Symptom Assessment Scale (ESAS), Karnofsky, ECOG.

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

Document with each clinical review. For non-communicating patients, use observational tools. For cognitively impaired, use PAIN-AD. For dementia, Abbey Pain Scale. For children, use age-appropriate (FLACC, Wong-Baker faces).

2. Management

Use consistent tool. Reassess regularly. Combine self-report (gold standard) with behavioural/physiological measures when needed. Document intervention-response. Total pain: physical + psychological + social + spiritual.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
VAS0-100mm or 0-10cm line
NRS0-10; most common
VRSNone/mild/moderate/severe
FacesWong-Baker; children
BPIBrief Pain Inventory; multidimensional
McGillMultidimensional; 78 descriptors
PAINADDementia, non-communicating
FLACCChildren
SeverityMild 1-3, moderate 4-6, severe 7-10
Re-assessAfter every intervention

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/ATools

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Best tool for adult? A: NRS (0-10) — simple, validated, sensitive.
  1. 2. Q: Best tool for dementia? A: PAIN-AD.
  1. 3. Q: Best tool for non-communicating ICU? A: Behavioural Pain Scale (BPS) or Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool (CPOT).
  1. 4. Q: Tool for breakthrough pain? A: Brief Pain Inventory (BPI) assesses both background and breakthrough.
  1. 5. Q: What is VAS? A: Visual Analogue Scale 0-100mm horizontal line, patient marks pain intensity.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

topics.title: Pain In Non Communicating And Dementia Patients Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Pain assessment in non-communicating patients (dementia, stroke, learning disability, intubated, end of life) is challenging as self-report is not possible. Use observational tools: PAINAD (Pain Assessment IN Advanced Dementia), Abbey Pain Scale, Doloplus-2 (elderly), FLACC (children), BPS (Behavioural Pain Scale, ICU). Behavioural indicators: facial expression (grimacing), vocalisation (moaning, calling out), body movement (guarding, rigidity), behaviour (agitation, aggression), physiological (tachycardia, hypertension).

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Pain in non-communicating and dementia patients is under-recognised and under-treated. Signs: facial expression (grimacing, frowning), vocalisation (groaning, moaning, calling out, asking for help), body language (guarding, rubbing, restlessness, rigidity), behavioural changes (aggression, agitation, withdrawal, sleep disturbance, refusal of care, decreased appetite), physiological (tachycardia, hypertension, sweating). Tools: PAIN-AD (Pain in Advanced Dementia, 5 items 0-10 each), Abbey Pain Scale (6 items), Doloplus-2, BPS (Behavioural Pain Scale for ICU), CPOT (Critical-Care Pain Observation Tool).

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

Behavioural and physiological observation; proxy report from carers/family; consider common pain triggers (positioning, hygiene, procedures, continence care). Disease-specific: full blood count, ESR, CRP, imaging for occult fracture, urinary retention (bladder scan), faecal impaction, oral thrush, pressure ulcers.

2. Management

Identify cause (thorough assessment using tools), treat cause, regular paracetamol (low threshold), regular opioid (if pain suspected, trial with careful titration and monitoring), topical (lignocaine patch, capsaicin for localised). Non-pharmacological: positioning, comfortable environment, music, touch, massage. Treat delirium, anxiety, depression. Family involvement, communication. Trial of analgesia with monitoring (N-of-1 trial).

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
PAINADPain Assessment IN Advanced Dementia; 5 items 0-2
Abbey6 items; 0-3 each
Doloplus-2Elderly; 10 items
FLACCChildren 2 months-7y; 5 items
BPSICU; 3 items
BehaviouralFacial, vocal, body movement
PhysiologicalHR, BP, RR
TrialTrial of analgesia if in doubt

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: How to assess pain in advanced dementia? A: PAIN-AD scale; behavioural observation; family/carer report.
  1. 2. Q: Why is pain under-treated in dementia? A: Communication difficulties, atypical presentation, attribution to disease/behavioural issues, lack of tools.
  1. 3. Q: Trial of analgesia principle? A: If pain suspected, treat and monitor; if no improvement, consider non-pain cause.
  1. 4. Q: Common under-recognised pain in dementia? A: Musculoskeletal, dental, constipation, urinary retention, pressure ulcers, fracture (especially post-fall).
  1. 5. Q: PAIN-AD components? A: Breathing, vocalisation, facial expression, body language, consolability.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Pain Physiology And Pathways Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Pain is defined by the IASP as 'an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with, or resembling that associated with, actual or potential tissue damage'. Pain pathways involve nociception (transduction), transmission, modulation, and perception. Nociceptors (Aδ and C fibres) detect noxious stimuli; transmission via dorsal horn → spinothalamic tract → thalamus → somatosensory cortex (sensory), limbic system (emotional). Modulation by descending pathways (periaqueductal grey → rostroventral medulla → dorsal horn), endogenous opioids, serotonin, noradrenaline. Central sensitisation (wind-up) amplifies pain in chronic states.

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Pain is a complex sensory and emotional experience with both nociceptive and neuropathic components. Nociceptors (free nerve endings) are activated by mechanical, thermal, and chemical stimuli, with Aδ fibres mediating fast, sharp, well-localised pain, and C fibres mediating slow, dull, burning, poorly-localised pain. Clinical features of acute pain: sympathetic activation (tachycardia, hypertension, sweating, dilated pupils), protective behaviour, muscle spasm, anxiety. Chronic pain (>3 months): maladaptation, depression, sleep disturbance, functional impairment, autonomic changes, often no sympathetic activation. Pain types: nociceptive (somatic — well-localised, sharp; visceral — diffuse, cramping, referred), neuropathic (shooting, burning, electric, with allodynia, hyperalgesia, dysaesthesia), mixed. Central sensitisation explains the wind-up phenomenon in chronic pain (NMDA-mediated, hyperexcitable dorsal horn neurons).

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

Clinical assessment with validated tools: NRS (numerical 0-10), VAS (visual analogue 0-100mm), VRS (verbal rating, none/mild/moderate/severe), McGill Pain Questionnaire, BPI (Brief Pain Inventory), LANSS (Leeds Assessment of Neuropathic Symptoms and Signs), DN4 (Douleur Neuropathique 4), PAIN-AD (Pain in Advanced Dementia). Disease-specific: imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI for bone/visceral/neuropathic), nerve conduction studies, blood tests (ESR, CRP, autoimmune, infection, metabolic), quantitative sensory testing (QST).

2. Management

Multimodal approach: treat underlying cause; total pain concept (physical + psychological + social + spiritual); WHO analgesic ladder with adjuvants for neuropathic; non-pharmacological (TENS, CBT, physiotherapy, acupuncture, exercise, mindfulness); interventional (nerve blocks, spinal cord stimulation, intrathecal pump); psychological support; address sleep, depression, anxiety; family/social support.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
IASPUnpleasant sensory and emotional experience
NociceptorsAδ (fast, sharp) and C (slow, dull) fibres
TransductionStimulus → electrical signal via ion channels (TRPV1, Nav1.7)
TransmissionDorsal horn → spinothalamic → thalamus → cortex
ModulationDescending: PAG, RVM, dorsal horn; endorphins, serotonin, NA
PerceptionSensory cortex (somatic), limbic (emotional), frontal (cognitive)
Central sensitisationWind-up; NMDA-mediated; chronic pain
Gate controlMelzack & Wall, 1965; large fibre activation inhibits small fibre
Endogenous opioidsEnkephalins, endorphins, dynorphins; μ, δ, κ receptors
NeuropathicLesion/disease of somatosensory system
NociceptiveActual or threatened tissue damage

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Nociceptive painSomatic/visceral; tissue damage; opioid-responsive
Neuropathic painNerve damage; burning/shooting; LANSS/DN4+
Nociplastic painNo tissue/nerve damage (e.g., fibromyalgia); central sensitisation
Psychogenic painPsychiatric; somatoform; no organic basis

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: What is the IASP definition of pain? A: An unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with, or resembling that associated with, actual or potential tissue damage.
  1. 2. Q: Which fibres mediate fast and slow pain? A: Aδ fibres = fast, sharp, well-localised; C fibres = slow, dull, burning.
  1. 3. Q: What is central sensitisation? A: NMDA-mediated hyperexcitability of dorsal horn neurons in chronic pain, leading to wind-up and allodynia.
  1. 4. Q: Name 4 ascending/descending pain pathways. A: Ascending: spinothalamic (lateral — pain/temp; medial — crude touch); spinoreticular; trigeminothalamic. Descending: PAG → rostroventral medulla → dorsal horn (serotonin, NA, endogenous opioids).
  1. 5. Q: What is the difference between nociceptive and neuropathic pain? A: Nociceptive = tissue damage, well-localised, opioid-responsive; neuropathic = nerve damage, burning/shooting, opioid-poorly-responsive, requires adjuvants (gabapentin, TCA, SNRI).

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. T-T-M-P = Transduction, Transmission, Modulation, Perception

topics.title: Total Pain Concept Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Pain Physiology & Assessment

1. 1. Definition

Total pain (Cicely Saunders) is the recognition that pain in advanced disease is not just physical but encompasses emotional, social, spiritual, and existential suffering. Components: physical (disease, treatment), emotional (depression, anxiety, fear), social (family, isolation, finances), spiritual (meaning, faith, hope), existential (loss, death). Assessment requires a multidisciplinary team. Treatment requires addressing all dimensions — pharmacological (analgesics) AND non-pharmacological (counselling, spiritual care, family support).

2. 2. Clinical Features and Presentation

Total pain concept (Cicely Saunders, founder of modern palliative care) recognises that pain has multiple components: physical (tissue damage, nerve, organ), psychological (anxiety, depression, fear, anger), social (loss of role, financial, isolation, family conflict), spiritual (existential distress, loss of meaning, fear of death). All components interact. Clinical features: complex pain, refractory to opioids alone, requires multimodal team, family involvement, addressing non-physical components is essential.

3. 3. Investigation and Management Summary

1. Investigations

Holistic assessment: physical, psychological, social, spiritual. Use total pain framework. Tools: HADS, ESAS, FICA, HOPE, PHQ-9, GAD-7. Multidisciplinary team assessment.

2. Management

Multimodal, multidisciplinary: physical (analgesia, adjuvants, interventional); psychological (CBT, counselling, relaxation, mindfulness); social (social work, family meetings, financial support, carer support); spiritual (chaplain, faith community, existential exploration). Family involvement. Address fears and concerns. Listen and validate.

4. 4. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
ConceptCicely Saunders; multidimensional pain
PhysicalDisease, treatment, immobility
EmotionalDepression, anxiety, fear, loss
SocialFamily burden, isolation, finances
SpiritualMeaning, faith, hope, despair
ExistentialLoss, death, identity, dignity
AssessmentMDT: medical, nursing, psychology, social work, chaplaincy
TreatmentPharmacological + non-pharmacological

5. 5. Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

6. 6. Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: What is the total pain concept? A: Pain with physical, psychological, social, and spiritual components, all interacting (Cicely Saunders).
  1. 2. Q: Why address all components? A: Pain in one component amplifies others; multimodal approach is more effective than opioids alone.
  1. 3. Q: Who is Cicely Saunders? A: Founder of modern palliative care, St Christopher's Hospice (1967), developed total pain concept.
  1. 4. Q: Example of social pain? A: Loss of role, financial worries, isolation, family conflict.
  1. 5. Q: Example of spiritual pain? A: Loss of meaning, fear of death, existential distress, loss of faith.

7. 7. Confusions and Mnemonics

topics.title: Advance Care Planning Acp Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Advance care planning (ACP) is a process of discussion between a patient, family/carers, and healthcare professionals about future care, particularly regarding end-of-life preferences. Components: ADRT (Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment) — legally binding, specifies treatments the patient would refuse in specific circumstances (e.g., CPR, ventilation, dialysis); lasting power of attorney (LPA) — legally designates a person to make decisions on behalf of the patient; values and preferences (place of care, level of intervention, resuscitation, organ donation, spiritual care). Documentation: written, dated, signed, witnessed, shared with relevant professionals (GP, hospital, ambulance, care home). Important: start early, review, address capacity, respect cultural/spiritual wishes, support family. MDT involvement.

Clinical Features and Presentation

ACP is process of discussion between patient, family, and healthcare professionals about future care, particularly end-of-life. Components: ADRT (Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment, legally binding), lasting power of attorney (LPA, designates decision-maker), values and preferences (place of care, level of intervention, resuscitation, organ donation, spiritual care). Documentation: written, dated, signed, witnessed, shared.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Capacity assessment (essential before ACP); mental capacity 4-criteria test.

Management

Start early, review, address capacity, respect cultural/spiritual wishes, family support, MDT involvement. ACP is a process, not an event. Capacity: must be assessed; if lacking, best-interests decision. ADRT: legally binding if valid and applicable. LPA: designate decision-maker for when capacity lacking. DNACPR: specific decision (not 'no treatment'). Family involvement per patient wishes. Documentation shared with relevant professionals (GP, hospital, ambulance, care home).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
ADRTAdvance Decision to Refuse Treatment; legally binding
LPALasting Power of Attorney (health and welfare)
ValuesPatient's preferences (place of care, level of intervention, spiritual)
CPR/DNACPRSpecific decisions about resuscitation
DocumentationWritten, dated, signed, witnessed, shared
Start earlyACP is a process, not an event; revisit
CapacityMust be assessed; if lacking, best-interests decision
FamilyInvolve (with consent), support
MDTDoctor, nurse, social worker, chaplain, etc.
ReviewIf circumstances change

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: ADRT? A: Advance Decision to Refuse Treatment; legally binding.
  1. 2. Q: LPA? A: Lasting Power of Attorney; health and welfare; designate decision-maker.
  1. 3. Q: When to start ACP? A: Early; before crisis; when patient has capacity.
  1. 4. Q: Capacity assessment? A: 4 criteria (understand, retain, weigh, communicate).
  1. 5. Q: Documentation? A: Written, dated, signed, witnessed, shared (GP, hospital, ambulance, care home).

Confusions and Mnemonics

topics.title: Bereavement Care And Grief Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Bereavement is the loss of a loved one through death. Grief is the natural response, with normal features (sadness, yearning, sleep/appetite changes, searching, numbness, guilt, anger, hallucinations of deceased). Stages/responses vary (Kübler-Ross: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance; not all experience all stages). Complicated grief (prolonged grief disorder): persistent for >12 months in adults (>6 months in children), functional impairment. Risk factors for complicated grief: sudden death, traumatic death, child loss, suicide, prior mental illness, social isolation. Support: normal grief does not require formal intervention, validate, listen, memorial, support groups; complicated grief — psychological therapy (grief-focused CBT, complicated grief therapy), antidepressants if comorbid depression. Family support: pre-death (anticipatory grief), at death, post-death (sympathy card, follow-up, referral to bereavement services).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Bereavement is loss of loved one through death. Normal grief: sadness, yearning, sleep/appetite changes, searching, numbness, guilt, anger, hallucinations of deceased. Stages: Kübler-Ross (denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance — not all experienced). Complicated grief (prolonged grief disorder): persistent >12 months in adults (>6 months in children), functional impairment.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Normal grief: no specific investigations. Complicated grief: assess severity, duration, impact.

Management

Normal grief: validate, listen, normalise, support groups, memorial. Complicated grief: grief-focused CBT, complicated grief therapy, antidepressants if comorbid depression. Risk factors for complicated: sudden death, traumatic, child loss, suicide, prior mental illness, social isolation. Family support: pre-death (anticipatory grief), at death, post-death (sympathy, follow-up, bereavement services).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Normal griefSadness, yearning, sleep/appetite changes, searching, guilt, anger, hallucinations
DurationMonths to years; individual variation
Kubler-RossDenial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance (not all stages experienced)
Complicated griefProlonged (>12 months adult, >6 months child), functional impairment
Risk factorsSudden, traumatic, child loss, suicide, prior mental illness, isolation
AnticipatoryPre-death grief in patient and family
SupportNormal: validate, listen, memorial, support groups
ComplicatedGrief-focused CBT, complicated grief therapy, antidepressants if depression
SympathyCard, follow-up, referral to bereavement services
ChildrenSpecial needs, age-appropriate language, support

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Normal griefWaves, can enjoy
DepressionPersistent anhedonia, suicidal ideation, biological features
Complicated grief>12 months, functional impairment

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Normal grief features? A: Sadness, yearning, sleep/appetite changes, searching, guilt, anger, hallucinations.
  1. 2. Q: Complicated grief? A: Persistent >12 months adult (>6 months child), functional impairment.
  1. 3. Q: Complicated grief treatment? A: Grief-focused CBT, complicated grief therapy, antidepressants if depression.
  1. 4. Q: Risk factors? A: Sudden, traumatic, child loss, suicide, prior mental illness, social isolation.
  1. 5. Q: Family support? A: Pre-death (anticipatory grief), at death (information, support), post-death (sympathy, follow-up, bereavement services).

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Bereavement = Normal vs complicated; CBT for complicated; family support throughout

topics.title: Breaking Bad News Spikes Protocol Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Breaking bad news is a core clinical communication skill, especially in palliative care. The SPIKES protocol (Baile et al., 2000) provides a structured approach: S (Setting — private, quiet, no interruptions, sit, silence phone), P (Perception — 'what do you understand about your illness?'), I (Invitation — 'how much information would you like?'), K (Knowledge — give information in small chunks, no jargon, check understanding), E (Emotions — respond with empathy: name emotion, validate, support), S (Strategy/Summary — plan next steps, summarise). Other models: BREAKS (Background, Rapport, Explore, Announce, Kindle, Summarise). Important: avoid jargon, allow silence, respond to emotions with empathy, do not give false reassurance, respect denial as a coping mechanism, document the conversation.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Breaking bad news is core clinical communication skill, especially in palliative care. SPIKES protocol (Baile et al., 2000): S (Setting), P (Perception), I (Invitation), K (Knowledge), E (Emotions), S (Strategy/Summary). Other: BREAKS, ABCDE.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

None.

Management

SPIKES: S (Setting — private, quiet, sitting, no interruptions, silence phone), P (Perception — 'what do you understand about your illness?'), I (Invitation — 'how much information would you like?'), K (Knowledge — small chunks, no jargon, check understanding), E (Emotions — empathy: name, validate, support), S (Strategy — plan, summary, follow-up). Other models: BREAKS (Background, Rapport, Explore, Announce, Kindle, Summarise). Avoid jargon, false reassurance, taking away hope. Respect denial as coping. Document conversation, who present, what said, plan. Family involvement per patient wishes.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
SPIKESSetting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy
SettingPrivate, quiet, sitting, no interruptions, silence phone
Perception'What do you understand about your illness?'
Invitation'How much information would you like?'
KnowledgeSmall chunks, no jargon, check understanding
EmotionsEmpathy: name, validate, support
StrategyPlan, summary, follow-up
AvoidJargon, false reassurance, giving too much at once, removing hope
DenialRespect as coping; revisit later
DocumentConversation, who present, what said, plan
FamilyInvolve per patient wishes

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/AFramework

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: SPIKES protocol? A: Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy.
  1. 2. Q: What is Perception? A: 'What do you understand about your illness?'
  1. 3. Q: What is Invitation? A: 'How much information would you like?'
  1. 4. Q: Empathy? A: Name, validate, support.
  1. 5. Q: Document? A: Conversation, who present, what said, plan, follow-up.

topics.title: Care Of The Dying Patient Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Care of the dying is the active, holistic care of patients in the last hours to days of life, with focus on comfort, dignity, and family support. Recognition of dying: bedbound, semi-comatose, only able to take sips, unable to take oral medications, deteriorating. Priorities: symptom control (pain, breathlessness, N&V, delirium, respiratory secretions, agitation, pruritus), hydration/nutrition (oral if possible, careful consideration of IV/SC fluids — may worsen secretions), psychological and spiritual support, family support, place of care. Anticipatory prescribing ('just in case' box): commonly morphine/diamorphine, midazolam, levomepromazine, haloperidol, glycopyrronium, hyoscine butylbromide. After-death care: verification, certification, bereavement support.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Care of the dying is active, holistic care of patients in last hours to days of life. Recognition of dying: bedbound, semi-comatose, only able to take sips, unable to take oral medications, deteriorating. Priorities: comfort, dignity, symptom control, family support. Symptoms in last days: pain, dyspnoea, N&V, delirium (terminal agitation), respiratory secretions (death rattle), pruritus, mouth care, incontinence.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Minimal investigations at end of life (cause focus on comfort); routine observations may be reduced. Identify and treat reversible (pain, urinary retention, constipation).

Management

Comfort measures: anticipatory prescribing ('just in case' box: opioid, midazolam, levomepromazine, haloperidol, glycopyrronium, hyoscine butylbromide); regular review; simple regimens; common routes (PO, SC, CSCI, PR, buccal); avoid polypharmacy. Hydration/nutrition: oral if possible, sips, ice chips, careful consideration of IV/SC fluids (may worsen secretions). Mouth care: sips, ice chips, lip balm, artificial saliva spray, treat oral Candida. Family support, information, involvement. Place of care (home, hospice, hospital), respect patient preference. After-death care: verification, certification, bereavement.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
RecognitionBedbound, semi-comatose, sips only, deteriorating, unable oral meds
PrioritiesComfort, dignity, symptom control, family support
SymptomsPain, dyspnoea, N&V, delirium, respiratory secretions, agitation, pruritus
HydrationOral care (sips, ice chips), careful consideration of IV/SC fluids
Anticipatory'Just in case' box: opioid, midazolam, levomepromazine, glycopyrronium
Place of careHome, hospice, hospital; respect patient preference
FamilyInformation, involvement, support after death
BereavementSympathy card, follow-up, professional support if needed
LCPLiverpool Care Pathway (replaced by NICE guidelines)
VerificationConfirm death by clinical examination (pulseless, apnoeic, no heart sounds, fixed pupils)
CertificationDeath certificate, MCCD (medical cause of death)
Sudden deathDifferent — support, sign MCCD, may need coroner/procurator fiscal

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Sudden death (cardiac arrest) vs expected deathDifferent pathway
Reversible deteriorationLook for cause if appropriate

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Recognition of dying? A: Bedbound, semi-comatose, sips only, deteriorating, unable oral meds.
  1. 2. Q: Anticipatory prescribing? A: 'Just in case' box: opioid (morphine 5mg SC), midazolam 5mg SC, levomepromazine 6.25mg, haloperidol 1.5mg, glycopyrronium 200mcg, hyoscine butylbromide 20mg.
  1. 3. Q: Mouth care? A: Sips, ice chips, lip balm, artificial saliva spray, treat Candida.
  1. 4. Q: Hydration? A: Oral if possible, careful consideration of IV/SC fluids (may worsen secretions), family discussion.
  1. 5. Q: Family support? A: Information, involvement, support, after-death (sympathy, follow-up, bereavement).

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Dnacpr And Treatment Escalation Decisions Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

DNACPR (Do Not Attempt Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) and treatment escalation plans (TEP) are decisions about appropriate level of intervention in deteriorating patients, balancing benefits, burdens, and patient preferences. CPR: chest compressions, ventilation, defibrillation, adrenaline; success rate in advanced illness <1-5%; can cause harm. Indications for DNACPR: patient wishes, futility (e.g., advanced cancer, end-stage organ failure), no benefit, quality of life. Decision: MDT discussion, patient (if capacity) and family involvement, documented, shared with relevant services. Treatment escalation: ward-based care, high-dependency, ICU, palliative. Important: do not equate DNACPR with no treatment — patient may still receive full active treatment except for CPR.

Clinical Features and Presentation

DNACPR (Do Not Attempt Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) and treatment escalation plans (TEP) are decisions about appropriate level of intervention, balancing benefits, burdens, and patient preferences. CPR: chest compressions, ventilation, defibrillation, adrenaline; success rate in advanced illness <1-5%; can cause harm.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

MDT discussion, patient (if capacity) and family, documentation, shared with services.

Management

Indications for DNACPR: patient wishes, futility (e.g., advanced cancer, end-stage organ failure), no benefit, quality of life. Decision: MDT, patient (if capacity), family, documented, shared. TEP: ward-based care, high-dependency, ICU, palliative. DNACPR not 'do not treat' — patient may receive full active treatment except for CPR. Communication: sensitive, honest, respect denial, support family. Documentation: form, electronic record, share with ambulance, out-of-hours. Review if circumstances change.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CPRCompressions, ventilation, defibrillation, adrenaline
SuccessOut-of-hospital ~5%; advanced cancer <1-5%
IndicationsPatient wishes, futility, no benefit, quality of life
DecisionMDT, patient (if capacity), family, documented, shared
DNACPRSpecific decision; not 'do not treat'
TEPTreatment escalation plan; ward/HDU/ICU/palliative
CommunicationSensitive, honest, respect denial, support family
DocumentationForm (DNACPR), electronic record, share with ambulance, out-of-hours
ReviewIf circumstances change
CapacityIf lacking, best-interests decision

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/ADecision

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Success of CPR in advanced illness? A: <1-5% (often <1% in advanced cancer).
  1. 2. Q: Indications for DNACPR? A: Patient wishes, futility, no benefit, quality of life.
  1. 3. Q: Decision process? A: MDT, patient (if capacity), family, documented, shared.
  1. 4. Q: DNACPR vs treatment? A: DNACPR is specific decision (no CPR); patient may still receive full active treatment otherwise.
  1. 5. Q: Documentation? A: DNACPR form, electronic record, share with ambulance, out-of-hours, care home.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. DNACPR = Patient wishes + futility + MDT + documented; not 'no treatment'

topics.title: End Of Life Ethical And Legal Frameworks Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

End-of-life ethical and legal frameworks guide decisions about withdrawing/withholding treatment, palliative sedation, euthanasia, and physician-assisted suicide. UK legal framework: Mental Capacity Act 2005 (capacity assessment, best interests, ADRT, lasting power of attorney), Suicide Act 1961 (assisting suicide is illegal in UK, but prosecution rarely pursued for compassionate cases), common law on withdrawing/withholding treatment (ethically and legally equivalent). International: euthanasia legal in Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, Colombia, Canada (MAID), Western Australia, Spain, New Zealand; PAS legal in some US states (Oregon, Washington, California, etc.), Switzerland (PASS), Germany. Medical consensus: palliative care is distinct from euthanasia/PAS; palliative sedation with appropriate intent is not euthanasia.

Clinical Features and Presentation

End-of-life ethical and legal frameworks guide decisions about withdrawing/withholding treatment, palliative sedation, euthanasia, PAS. UK legal framework: Mental Capacity Act 2005 (capacity, best interests, ADRT, LPA), Suicide Act 1961 (assisting suicide illegal in UK), common law on withdrawing/withholding.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

None (clinical/legal).

Management

UK: Mental Capacity Act 2005, Suicide Act 1961, common law. International: euthanasia legal in Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, Colombia, Canada (MAID), Western Australia, Spain, New Zealand; PAS legal in some US states, Switzerland, Germany. Palliative care is distinct from euthanasia/PAS. Capacity: assessment; if lacking, best-interests decision. ADRT: legally binding if valid and applicable. LPA: designate decision-maker for when capacity lacking. MDT: ethics committee, senior review, family, documentation.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
UKMental Capacity Act 2005 (capacity, best interests, ADRT, LPA)
Suicide Act1961, assisting suicide illegal but rarely prosecuted compassionately
Withholding/withdrawingEthically and legally equivalent (UK common law)
EuthanasiaLegal in NL, BE, LU, CO, CA, WA, ES, NZ
PASLegal in some US states, CH, DE
Palliative careDistinct from euthanasia/PAS
CapacityPatient capacity for decisions
Best interestsMulti-disciplinary, patient wishes, family input
ADRTLegally binding if valid and applicable
LPADesignate decision-maker when capacity lacking

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/AFramework

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: UK framework? A: Mental Capacity Act 2005 (capacity, best interests, ADRT, LPA); Suicide Act 1961.
  1. 2. Q: Where is euthanasia legal? A: Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, Colombia, Canada (MAID), Western Australia, Spain, New Zealand.
  1. 3. Q: Where is PAS legal? A: Some US states (Oregon, Washington, California), Switzerland, Germany.
  1. 4. Q: Withholding vs withdrawing? A: Ethically and legally equivalent (UK common law).
  1. 5. Q: Palliative care vs euthanasia? A: Distinct; palliative care never hastens death intentionally.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Ethical Issues In Palliative Care Double Effect Withholding Treatment Euthanasia Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Ethical issues in palliative care include: doctrine of double effect (palliative care principle — if an intervention (e.g., high-dose opioid) has two effects, one good (pain relief) and one bad (potential respiratory depression, possible hastening of death), it is morally acceptable if the intention is the good effect, the action is not intrinsically wrong, and the bad effect is not the means to the good); withholding vs withdrawing treatment (both ethically equivalent, withdrawing often more difficult emotionally but legally equivalent); euthanasia (active, intentional ending of life on request, illegal in UK and most countries, distinct from palliative sedation or withholding treatment); physician-assisted suicide (PAS, legal in some jurisdictions — Switzerland, Netherlands, Belgium, US states Oregon/Washington). MDT, patient, family discussions; ethics committee; documentation.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Ethical issues in palliative care: doctrine of double effect (intervention with two effects — good relief, bad potential harm; intention good, morally acceptable), withholding vs withdrawing treatment (ethically equivalent), euthanasia (active intentional ending of life on request, illegal in UK), physician-assisted suicide (PAS, legal in some jurisdictions).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

None (clinical/ethical).

Management

Doctrine of double effect: intention matters, both effects known, good effect not means to bad; allows high-dose opioids, palliative sedation. Withholding: not starting treatment (e.g., ventilation). Withdrawing: stopping treatment; ethically equivalent to withholding. Euthanasia: active intentional ending on request; illegal UK. PAS: physician-assisted suicide; legal in some jurisdictions (Switzerland, Netherlands, Belgium, US states). Palliative sedation: not euthanasia. Capacity: patient capacity for decisions. MDT: multidisciplinary team; ethics committee. Documentation: decisions, discussions, reasoning, family.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Double effectTwo effects: good (relief) and bad (potential harm); intention is good
ExamplesHigh-dose opioid (relief + respiratory depression), palliative sedation (relief + unconsciousness)
WithholdingNot starting treatment (e.g., ventilation)
WithdrawingStopping treatment; ethically equivalent to withholding
EuthanasiaActive intentional ending of life on request; illegal UK
PASPhysician-assisted suicide; legal in some jurisdictions
Palliative sedationReducing consciousness for refractory symptoms; not euthanasia
CapacityPatient capacity for decision-making
MDTMultidisciplinary team; ethics committee if needed
DocumentationDecisions, discussions, reasoning, family involvement

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Doctrine of double effect? A: Two effects (good and bad); intention is good; not the means to bad.
  1. 2. Q: Examples in pall care? A: High-dose opioid (pain relief + potential RD), palliative sedation (relief + unconsciousness).
  1. 3. Q: Withholding vs withdrawing? A: Ethically equivalent.
  1. 4. Q: Euthanasia in UK? A: Illegal.
  1. 5. Q: Palliative sedation vs euthanasia? A: Sedation reduces consciousness for refractory symptoms; intention is relief; not euthanasia.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Double effect = intention matters, both effects known, good effect not means to bad

topics.title: Malignant Bowel Obstruction Mbo Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Malignant bowel obstruction is mechanical or functional obstruction of the bowel in patients with advanced cancer, most commonly ovarian, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, or small bowel cancer. Causes: mechanical (intrinsic tumour, adhesions, extrinsic compression, hernia, intussusception) and functional (paraneoplastic pseudo-obstruction, opioid-induced, autonomic dysfunction). Clinical features: abdominal pain (colicky, then continuous), distension, vomiting (early in high, late in low obstruction), constipation/obstipation, absent flatus. Diagnosis: clinical + abdominal X-ray (dilated loops, air-fluid levels, gasless), CT abdomen to determine site/cause. Management: surgery (consider if prognosis >2-3 months, single level, performance status OK), medical (analgesia — opioid + anticholinergic hyoscine butylbromide; antiemetic — cyclizine, haloperidol, levomepromazine; antisecretory — hyoscine butylbromide, glycopyrronium, octreotide for inoperable; NG tube for vomiting, IV fluids for dehydration, dexamethasone to reduce oedema). Palliative stenting (colorectal, gastric outlet).

Clinical Features and Presentation

MBO is mechanical or functional obstruction in advanced cancer (ovarian, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, small bowel). Causes: tumour, adhesions, paraneoplastic, opioid, anticholinergic. Symptoms: pain (colicky, then continuous), distension, vomiting (early in high, late in low), constipation, absent flatus.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical + AXR (dilated loops, air-fluid levels) + CT (site, level, cause).

Management

Surgery: consider if prognosis >2-3 months, single level, good PS. Medical: analgesia (opioid + anticholinergic hyoscine butylbromide), antiemetic (cyclizine, haloperidol, levomepromazine — avoid metoclopramide in mechanical), antisecretory (hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC, glycopyrronium 800mcg/24h, octreotide 250-500mcg/24h), dexamethasone 6-8mg IV, NG tube (vomiting), IV fluids (cautiously), stenting (colorectal, gastric outlet), venting G-tube.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesMechanical (tumour, adhesions, hernia), functional (paralytic, drug)
CancersOvarian, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, small bowel
SymptomsPain (colicky), distension, vomiting, constipation, absent flatus
DiagnosisClinical + AXR (dilated loops, air-fluid levels) + CT
SurgeryConsider if prognosis >2-3 months, single level, good PS
AnalgesiaOpioid (morphine) + anticholinergic (hyoscine butylbromide)
AntiemeticCyclizine, haloperidol, levomepromazine
AntisecretoryHyoscine butylbromide, glycopyrronium, octreotide
DexamethasoneReduce oedema, antiemetic
NG tubeFor persistent vomiting
StentingPalliative (colorectal, gastric outlet)
Venting G-tubePEG venting if gastric outlet obstruction

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Mechanical vs functionalDrug-induced, paraneoplastic
Non-malignant obstructionAdhesion, hernia
ConstipationFaecal loading

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line medical? A: Analgesia (opioid + anticholinergic), antiemetic (cyclizine, haloperidol), antisecretory (hyoscine, glycopyrronium, octreotide), dexamethasone.
  1. 2. Q: Why avoid metoclopramide? A: Prokinetic; worsens colic, contraindicated in mechanical obstruction.
  1. 3. Q: Octreotide? A: Inoperable MBO; reduces secretions and pain.
  1. 4. Q: Surgery consideration? A: Single level, good PS, prognosis >2-3 months.
  1. 5. Q: Dexamethasone? A: Reduces bowel wall oedema, may relieve obstruction partially, antiemetic.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Symptom Control In Last Days Of Life Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Symptom control in the last days of life is the active management of pain, dyspnoea, nausea/vomiting, respiratory secretions (death rattle), delirium, terminal agitation, pruritus, mouth care, and family support. Most patients will have multiple symptoms. Key principles: anticipatory prescribing (just in case), regular review, simple regimens, common routes (PO, SC, CSCI, PR, buccal), avoid polypharmacy, address family/carer concerns, keep patient clean, comfortable, and dignified. Mouth care: sips, ice chips, lip balm, artificial saliva spray, treat oral candida. Respiratory secretions (death rattle): noisy breathing from pooled secretions; treat with anticholinergics (glycopyrronium 200-400µg SC, hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Most dying patients have multiple symptoms requiring active management: pain, dyspnoea, N&V, respiratory secretions (death rattle), delirium (terminal agitation), terminal agitation, pruritus, mouth care, family support.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Minimal (focus on comfort); identify and treat reversible.

Management

Anticipatory prescribing ('just in case' box: opioid, midazolam, levomepromazine, haloperidol, glycopyrronium). Pain: regular opioid (PO or SC/CSCI), breakthrough plan. Dyspnoea: opioid, benzodiazepine, oxygen, positioning, airflow. N&V: levomepromazine, haloperidol, cyclizine, metoclopramide, ondansetron. Death rattle: glycopyrronium 200-400mcg SC, hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC. Delirium: haloperidol, levomepromazine. Agitation: midazolam (consider palliative sedation). Pruritus: antihistamine, opioid switch. Mouth care: sips, ice chips, lip balm, treat Candida. Family: information, support, involvement, after-death.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
PainRegular opioid (PO or SC/CSCI), breakthrough plan
DyspnoeaOpioid (morphine), benzodiazepine (midazolam), positioning, airflow
N&VLevomepromazine (broad), haloperidol, cyclizine, metoclopramide
Death rattleNoisy breathing; glycopyrronium 200-400µg SC, hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC
DeliriumHaloperidol, levomepromazine
AgitationMidazolam (consider palliative sedation)
PruritusAntihistamine, opioid switch (especially morphine → fentanyl)
Mouth careSips, ice chips, lip balm, artificial saliva, treat Candida
Anticipatory'Just in case' box: opioid, midazolam, antiemetic, glycopyrronium
FamilyInformation, support, involvement

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Reversible causesPain, retention, metabolic, drugs

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Anticipatory prescribing? A: 'Just in case' box: opioid, midazolam, antiemetic, glycopyrronium.
  1. 2. Q: Death rattle? A: Glycopyrronium 200-400mcg SC, hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC.
  1. 3. Q: Delirium at end of life? A: Haloperidol 1-3mg SC; levomepromazine; family education, comfort care.
  1. 4. Q: Mouth care? A: Sips, ice chips, lip balm, artificial saliva, treat Candida.
  1. 5. Q: Family support? A: Information, involvement, support, after-death (sympathy, follow-up).

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Last days = Anticipatory prescribing + common route + simple regimen + family support

topics.title: Syringe Drivers Csci Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — End of Life Care

Definition

Continuous subcutaneous infusion (CSCI, 'syringe driver') is a portable pump delivering subcutaneous infusion over 24 hours, used in palliative care when oral route is not possible (dysphagia, vomiting, weakness, intestinal obstruction). Drugs commonly used: morphine/diamorphine, midazolam, haloperidol, levomepromazine, cyclizine, glycopyrronium, hyoscine butylbromide, dexamethasone, metoclopramide, ondansetron. Compatibility: limited — usually 2-3 drugs in one syringe; check compatibility tables (most common are diamorphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium, or diamorphine + haloperidol + midazolam). Site: anterior abdominal wall, thigh, upper arm; rotate every 3-5 days; can use chest if edematous. Volume: usually 10-20mL over 24h.

Clinical Features and Presentation

CSCI (continuous subcutaneous infusion, 'syringe driver') is portable pump delivering SC infusion over 24h, used in palliative care when oral route not possible. Drugs commonly used: morphine/diamorphine, midazolam, haloperidol, levomepromazine, cyclizine, glycopyrronium, hyoscine butylbromide, dexamethasone, metoclopramide, ondansetron.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Compatibility (check tables; usually 2-3 drugs in one syringe); site (anterior abdominal wall, thigh, upper arm; rotate 3-5 days); volume (10-20mL over 24h).

Management

Compatibility: e.g., diamorphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium. Site: anterior abdominal wall, thigh, upper arm; rotate every 3-5 days. Volume: 10-20mL over 24h. Diamorphine: more water-soluble than morphine, preferred for SC. Glycopyrronium: anticholinergic, no CNS penetration, no sedation. Site reaction: common; rotate; consider alternative drug or route. Syringe driver for symptom control, not for hydration.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
IndicationOral not possible (dysphagia, vomiting, weakness, obstruction, dying)
SiteAnterior abdominal wall, thigh, upper arm; rotate 3-5 days
Common drugsDiamorphine/morphine, midazolam, haloperidol, levomepromazine, cyclizine, glycopyrronium
Compatibility2-3 drugs max; check tables (e.g., diamorphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium)
Volume10-20mL over 24h
DiamorphineMore water-soluble than morphine, preferred for SC (volume)
GlycopyrroniumAnticholinergic, no CNS penetration, no sedation (vs hyoscine)
Trouble-shootCheck site, needle position, pump function, drug stability
Site reactionCommon; rotate; consider alternative drug or route
HydrationSyringe driver for symptom control, not for hydration (sub-Q fluid absorption limited)

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
N/AMethod

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Common CSCI drug combination? A: Diamorphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium.
  1. 2. Q: Why diamorphine? A: More water-soluble than morphine, smaller volume for SC.
  1. 3. Q: Site? A: Anterior abdominal wall, thigh, upper arm; rotate every 3-5 days.
  1. 4. Q: Volume? A: 10-20mL over 24h.
  1. 5. Q: Trouble-shoot? A: Check site, needle position, pump function, drug stability, compatibility.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. CSCI = Dying/PO not possible; diamorphine + midazolam + glycopyrronium common
