topics.title: Opioid Side Effects Constipation Nv Respiratory Depression Sedation Pruritus Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Opioid Management

Definition

Opioid side effects are common and predictable from μ-receptor activity: constipation (gut; prophylactic laxatives), nausea and vomiting (CTZ; first 1-2 weeks, antiemetics), sedation (CNS; tolerance develops), respiratory depression (brainstem; late, life-threatening; naloxone), pruritus (μ on mast cells; antihistamines), miosis (Edinger-Westphal), urinary retention, dry mouth, sweating, OIH, hypogonadism. Most tolerance: N&V, sedation. Least tolerance: constipation. Most dangerous: respiratory depression. Treatment: prophylactic laxatives (all opioid patients), antiemetic first 1-2 weeks, naloxone for severe RD, opioid rotation if persistent.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Opioid side effects are common and expected; tolerance develops to most (except constipation). Constipation: most common, no tolerance, all opioids; prevention mandatory (laxative). Nausea and vomiting: 30-50% initially, tolerance develops over 1-2 weeks; treat with antiemetic (haloperidol, metoclopramide, ondansetron). Respiratory depression: late sign, rare with oral stable doses; naloxone 3-5mcg IV, repeat (lower dose to avoid withdrawal and pain crisis). Sedation: common initial, tolerance develops; if persistent, reduce dose or rotate. Pruritus: histamine-mediated (more with morphine), tolerance; treat with antihistamine, opioid rotation (fentanyl, methadone).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Baseline: pain, sedation (Pasero scale), respiratory rate, bowel function, N&V, pruritus. Routine monitoring. Naloxone challenge if RD suspected.

Management

Prevention is key: co-prescribe stimulant laxative (senna) + softener (docusate); antiemetic prn first 1-2 weeks. Treat side effects: constipation (osmotic laxative, opioid rotation), N&V (antiemetic, opioid rotation), RD (naloxone, opioid reduction/rotation), sedation (reduce dose, caffeine, rotation), pruritus (antihistamine, opioid rotation, ondansetron).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
ConstipationProphylactic laxative (stool softener + stimulant)
N&VFirst 1-2 weeks; antiemetic (cyclizine, haloperidol, ondansetron)
SedationTolerance develops
Respiratory depressionLate, life-threatening; naloxone
PruritusAntihistamines, opioid rotation
MiosisEdinger-Westphal
OIHOpioid-induced hyperalgesia
ToleranceMost: N&V, sedation; least: constipation
Most dangerousRespiratory depression

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Disease progressionWorsening pain
Other cause of side effectMetabolic, sepsis, etc.

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Most common opioid side effect without tolerance? A: Constipation.
  1. 2. Q: Naloxone dose for RD? A: 3-5mcg IV, repeat (titrate to resp rate, not conscious level).
  1. 3. Q: How to manage opioid-induced constipation? A: Stimulant laxative (senna) + softener (docusate); osmotic (macrogol); rotate opioid; consider methylnaltrexone SC for refractory.
  1. 4. Q: Side effect with no tolerance? A: Constipation (need prophylactic laxative).
  1. 5. Q: Side effect with quick tolerance? A: Nausea, vomiting, sedation, pruritus (usually resolve in 1-2 weeks).

topics.title: Opioid Tolerance Dependence Addiction Pseudo Addiction Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Opioid Management

Definition

Four related but distinct concepts: (1) Tolerance: pharmacological right-shift of dose-response, requiring higher doses for same effect. (2) Physical dependence: physiological adaptation causing withdrawal on abrupt cessation/reduction. (3) Addiction (psychological dependence): compulsive use despite harm, loss of control, cravings, continued use despite negative consequences. (4) Pseudo-addiction: behavioural changes (clock-watching, requesting more medication) due to UNDER-treated pain that resolves with adequate analgesia. Patients with chronic pain on long-term opioids are often tolerant and physically dependent but NOT addicted.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Definitions: Tolerance: physiological adaptation, decreased effect of same dose over time; reversed by dose increase. Physical dependence: withdrawal on abrupt discontinuation or dose reduction; not addiction. Addiction (substance use disorder): psychological dependence, compulsive use, harm despite consequences; rare in pain patients (less than 0.1% without prior history). Pseudo-addiction: drug-seeking behaviour due to inadequate analgesia; resolves with adequate dose. Distinguishing is important to avoid undertreating pain.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History of substance use, opioid use (prescribed, OTC, illicit), behaviour (e.g., 'clock-watching', multiple prescriptions), functional impact.

Management

Tolerance: increase dose (cancer pain, progressive disease). Physical dependence: taper slowly to avoid withdrawal. Addiction: refer to addiction services, consider opioid-sparing, methadone/buprenorphine (addiction indication). Pseudo-addiction: increase dose. Use universal precautions (screening, agreements, monitoring, prescription drug monitoring).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
TolerancePharmacological; right-shift of dose-response
DependencePhysical; withdrawal on stop/reduce
AddictionCompulsive use despite harm, loss of control
Pseudo-addictionBehaviour from under-treated pain
Resolves withAdequate analgesia
Clock-watchingPseudo-addiction behaviour
Chronic painTolerance + dependence ≠ addiction

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Pseudo-addictionBehaviour from undertreated pain
AddictionCompulsive, harm, persists despite relief

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Definition of tolerance? A: Physiological adaptation requiring increased dose for same effect.
  1. 2. Q: Difference dependence vs addiction? A: Dependence = physical withdrawal; addiction = psychological, compulsive use, harm.
  1. 3. Q: Risk of opioid addiction in pain patients? A: Less than 0.1% without prior substance use history.
  1. 4. Q: Pseudo-addiction? A: Drug-seeking due to inadequate pain relief; resolves with adequate dose.
  1. 5. Q: Universal precautions? A: Screening, opioid agreement, urine drug screen, prescription monitoring, careful titration, storage/disposal education.

topics.title: Opioid Induced Hyperalgesia Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Opioid Management

Definition

Opioid-induced hyperalgesia (OIH) is a state of heightened pain sensitivity caused by prolonged opioid exposure, characterised by diffuse pain that is more widespread and severe than the original pain, and not relieved by opioid escalation. Mechanism: NMDA receptor activation, descending facilitation, glial activation, dynorphin release. Differentiate from tolerance (improves with escalation) and disease progression. Treatment: opioid reduction (20-30%), opioid rotation, NMDA antagonists (ketamine), non-opioid analgesics, multidisciplinary approach.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Opioid-induced hyperalgesia (OIH) is increased pain sensitivity from opioid use (prolonged/high dose), distinct from tolerance. Features: diffuse, poorly localised pain beyond original site, allodynia, hyperalgesia, often worsening despite dose escalation. Mechanism: NMDA receptor activation, central sensitisation, neuroinflammation. Risk factors: high dose, prolonged use, rapid escalation, genetic predisposition.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Suspect when: pain worsens despite increasing dose, pain beyond original site, allodynia/hyperalgesia. Rule out disease progression. Opioid rotation helps distinguish (rotation improves OIH but not tolerance).

Management

Reduce opioid dose (10-25% per week) or rotate to alternative (methadone has NMDA antagonist; lower OIH). NMDA antagonists: ketamine (oral, IV, intranasal), dextromethorphan. Non-opioid analgesia: paracetamol, NSAIDs, adjuvants (gabapentin, TCAs). Address central sensitisation.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
DefinitionHeightened pain sensitivity from prolonged opioid
MechanismNMDA, descending facilitation, glial, dynorphin
FeatureDiffuse, widespread, severe; not relieved by escalation
Vs toleranceWorsens with escalation
Vs progressionNo disease change
TreatmentOpioid reduction (20-30%), rotation
NMDAKetamine
MultidisciplinaryCBT, exercise, psychology
RiskHigh dose (>200mg OM/day), rapid escalation
ManagementRecognise early, reduce, rotate

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
ToleranceSame pain, need more
Disease progressionNew pain at site, not beyond
WithdrawalSymptoms other than pain

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: OIH vs tolerance? A: Tolerance = same pain, need more opioid; OIH = increased pain sensitivity (worse with more opioid).
  1. 2. Q: Mechanism? A: NMDA receptor activation, central sensitisation, neuroinflammation.
  1. 3. Q: Management? A: Reduce dose, rotate to methadone (NMDA antagonist), ketamine, adjuvants.
  1. 4. Q: How distinguish? A: Rotate opioid; if improves = OIH; if worse = tolerance/progression.
  1. 5. Q: Risk factors? A: High dose, prolonged use, rapid escalation.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. OIH = diffuse + more with opioid (paradox)
  2. Tolerance = right shift, improves with more; OIH = diffuse, worse with more
  3. OIH management = reduce + rotate + ketamine

topics.title: Anorexia Cachexia Syndrome Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Cancer cachexia is a multifactorial syndrome of involuntary weight loss, muscle wasting, anorexia, and metabolic disturbance in advanced illness (cancer, heart failure, COPD, AIDS). Pathogenesis: pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), tumour-derived factors, hormonal changes. Treatment: identify and treat reversible causes, nutritional support (enteral if possible, parenteral only in select), appetite stimulants (corticosteroids — short-term, dexamethasone; megestrol acetate; mirtazapine), exercise, treat depression. Prognosis: poor, often irreversible in late stages.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Anorexia-cachexia syndrome is a complex metabolic syndrome in advanced illness with weight loss, muscle wasting, anorexia, fatigue, weakness, decreased QoL. Mechanism: inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), catabolic state, decreased intake. Affects 50-80% of advanced cancer, end-stage COPD/CHF/HIV. May be primary (cachexia of disease) or secondary (inadequate intake — depression, dysphagia, nausea, dysgeusia, drugs). Reversible causes should be sought and treated.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Weight, BMI, percentage weight loss, mid-arm circumference, handgrip strength. Bloods: albumin, prealbumin, CRP, FBC, LFT, renal, glucose, TFT. Dietitian assessment. CT/MRI for muscle mass (if precise).

Management

Identify and treat reversible (depression, nausea, oral thrush, constipation, dysgeusia, drugs). Appetite stimulants: megestrol acetate 160-800mg/day (corticosteroid effect), mirtazapine 15-30mg nocte (antidepressant, weight gain), corticosteroids (dexamethasone 2-4mg/day, prednisolone 15-30mg/day — short-term, 2-4 weeks). Nutritional support: dietitian, oral supplements, enteral (NG/PEG) if adequate function, parenteral (TPN — rarely appropriate, late-stage). Family education (refocus on quality, not food).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
TypeMultifactorial wasting in advanced illness
CauseCytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), tumour factors
FeaturesWeight loss, muscle wasting, anorexia, fatigue
TreatmentIdentify cause, nutrition, appetite stimulant
CorticosteroidDexamethasone (short-term)
MegestrolProgestogen appetite stimulant
MirtazapineAnorexia, depression, sleep
PrognosisPoor, often irreversible

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Reversible causesDepression, nausea, dysphagia
End-stageOften irreversible

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Anorexia-cachexia mechanism? A: Inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6); metabolic; decreased intake.
  1. 2. Q: First-line appetite stimulant? A: Megestrol acetate (corticosteroid progestogen effect, 160-800mg/day) or mirtazapine.
  1. 3. Q: Corticosteroid for anorexia? A: Yes, dexamethasone 2-4mg/day or prednisolone 15-30mg/day, short-term (2-4 weeks); benefit fatigue, appetite, pain.
  1. 4. Q: Reversible causes? A: Depression, nausea, dysphagia, oral candida, constipation, drugs, dysgeusia.
  1. 5. Q: TPN in palliative care? A: Rarely; usually fails to improve outcomes in late-stage disease; may burden patient.

topics.title: Anxiety In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Anxiety is common in palliative care, especially with advanced disease, facing death, uncontrolled symptoms, and existential distress. Features: excessive worry, fear, restlessness, autonomic arousal, panic. Causes: existential (death, loss of control), physical (uncontrolled pain, dyspnoea, N&V), drugs (corticosteroids, withdrawal), psychiatric (pre-existing anxiety). Treatment: address causes (existential, spiritual, social); psychological (CBT, mindfulness, relaxation, counselling); pharmacological (short-term benzodiazepines: lorazepam 0.5-1mg, midazolam 2-5mg SC, diazepam; SSRI/SNRI for chronic).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Anxiety in palliative care is common (30-50%), persistent worry, fear, restlessness, panic, autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, sweating, hyperventilation), sleep disturbance, muscle tension. Causes: situational (cancer diagnosis, death anxiety, separation), medical (hyperthyroid, PE, heart failure, hypoxia, sepsis, drug withdrawal, drug-induced — steroids, stimulants), psychiatric (anxiety disorder, depression). Often comorbid with pain, depression, dyspnoea.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History, HADS or GAD-7, medical evaluation (oxygen saturation, TFT, drug review, ABG if indicated).

Management

Non-pharm: CBT, relaxation, mindfulness, breathing techniques, massage, music, spiritual support, family involvement, environmental modification. Pharmacological: SSRIs (long-term, generalised anxiety disorder — 4-6 weeks onset), short-acting benzodiazepines (acute symptoms, anticipatory anxiety, procedure anxiety): lorazepam 0.5-1mg SL/PO, diazepam 2-5mg PO, midazolam 2-5mg SC prn or CSCI; beta-blockers (autonomic symptoms); atypical antipsychotics if refractory; gabapentin/pregabalin (anxiety, also pain).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseExistential, physical, drugs, psychiatric
FeaturesWorry, fear, restlessness, autonomic
TreatmentAddress cause; psychological; pharmacological
PsychologicalCBT, mindfulness, relaxation
BenzodiazepineLorazepam, midazolam
SSRI/SNRIFor chronic

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
DeliriumAcute, fluctuating
Drug effect (steroids, stimulants)Temporal relationship
HyperthyroidTFT, weight loss
Panic disorderRecurrent attacks

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line for generalised anxiety? A: SSRI (escitalopram, sertraline) ± short-acting benzodiazepine for acute symptoms.
  1. 2. Q: First-line for acute anxiety in palliative care? A: Short-acting benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.5-1mg SL, midazolam 2-5mg SC, diazepam 2-5mg PO).
  1. 3. Q: Anxiety at end of life? A: Midazolam CSCI 5-20mg/24h.
  1. 4. Q: Beta-blocker in anxiety? A: For autonomic symptoms (tachycardia, tremor) in performance anxiety or specific situations.
  1. 5. Q: Common causes in palliative care? A: Cancer-related fear, death anxiety, uncontrolled symptoms, drug-induced, withdrawal, hypoxia, hyperthyroid.

topics.title: Breathlessness Dyspnoea Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Breathlessness (dyspnoea) is a subjective experience of breathing discomfort, common in palliative care (50-70% cancer, 90% COPD/heart failure). Causes: lung primary/secondary cancer, pleural effusion, lymphangitic carcinomatosis, SVCO, infection, anaemia, anxiety, heart failure, COPD, PE. Treatment: identify and treat reversible causes (effusion drainage, infection, anaemia transfusion, SVCO stent); non-pharmacological (positioning, cool airflow, relaxation); opioids (morphine 2.5-5mg q4h PO or 5-10mg/24h SC — reduces breathlessness without significant respiratory depression); anxiolytics (midazolam 2-5mg SC PRN, especially in last days); oxygen if hypoxic; fan (cool air).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Breathlessness in advanced illness affects 50-70% of patients with lung cancer, end-stage COPD/CHF, end-stage renal/liver disease, neuromuscular disease. Subjective experience of breathing discomfort, often disproportionate to objective measures. Causes: respiratory (lung cancer, COPD, pneumonia, PE, pleural effusion, lymphangitis, SVC), cardiac (CCF, anaemia, PE), metabolic (acidosis, anaemia), anxiety. Worst at end of life. May be episodic or chronic.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History (triggers, timing, position), examination (respiratory rate, effort, oxygen saturation, lung sounds, JVP, peripheral oedema, anaemia), bloods (FBC, BNP, ABG, glucose, U&E), CXR, ECG, BNP, oxygen saturation, peak flow if asthma/COPD.

Management

Treat reversible (infection, effusion, anaemia, bronchospasm, anxiety, SVC stent). Non-pharmacological: positioning (upright, fan, open window), cool airflow, breathing techniques, relaxation, pacing. Pharmacological: opioids (oral morphine 2.5-5mg q4-6h, or SC 1-2.5mg prn; CSCI if persistent — morphine or diamorphine); benzodiazepines for anxiety (midazolam 2-5mg SC prn, CSCI 5-20mg/24h; diazepam 2-5mg PO); oxygen if hypoxic (SpO2 <88%, but no evidence of benefit if not hypoxic).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseLung, pleural, lymphangitic, SVCO, anaemia, anxiety
TreatmentTreat cause; non-pharm; opioids
OpioidMorphine 2.5-5mg q4h PO
MechanismReduces central perception
MidazolamAnxiety component
OxygenIf hypoxic
FanCool airflow
AnxietyAddress it

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
PulmonaryCancer, COPD, infection, PE, effusion, SVC
CardiacCCF, anaemia, PE
MetabolicAcidosis, anaemia
AnxietyOften comorbid

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line drug for dyspnoea? A: Opioid (morphine 2.5-5mg PO q4-6h or SC 1-2.5mg prn).
  1. 2. Q: Mechanism of opioid in dyspnoea? A: Reduces perception of breathlessness, decreases central response to hypoxia/hypercapnia.
  1. 3. Q: Non-pharm for dyspnoea? A: Positioning, fan, open window, breathing techniques, relaxation.
  1. 4. Q: When to use oxygen? A: SpO2 <88%, or arterial hypoxaemia; no evidence of benefit in non-hypoxic breathlessness.
  1. 5. Q: First-line for anxiety component? A: Benzodiazepine (midazolam SC prn or CSCI, or diazepam PO).

topics.title: Constipation In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Constipation in palliative care is common (50-90% with advanced cancer). Causes: opioids (universal effect, prophylactic laxatives essential), decreased mobility, low fibre/fluid intake, drugs (5-HT3 antagonists, anticholinergics, opioids), metabolic (hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia), bowel obstruction. Bristol Stool Chart 1-2 = constipation. Treatment: prophylactic laxatives for all opioid patients; stimulant (senna) + softener (docusate); escalating to macrogol, lactulose, opioid receptor antagonists (methylnaltrexone, naloxegol — peripheral, for opioid-induced constipation), rectal (suppositories, enemas) if oral fails.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Constipation in palliative care is common (50-90%), reduced bowel frequency, hard stools, straining, sensation of incomplete evacuation, abdominal discomfort, distension. Causes: opioid (most common, all opioids, no tolerance), other drugs (5-HT3 antagonists, anticholinergics, diuretics, iron, calcium), decreased mobility, poor intake, dehydration, metabolic (hypothyroid, hypercalcaemia), mechanical (obstruction by tumour), neurological (spinal cord compression). Complications: overflow diarrhoea, faecal impaction, urinary retention, delirium, pseudo-obstruction, bowel perforation.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History (frequency, consistency, Bristol stool scale), examination (abdomen — distension, masses, bowel sounds; PR — stool, masses, impaction, anal tone), abdominal X-ray (faecal loading, obstruction), bloods (Ca, TSH, glucose, K).

Management

Prevention: co-prescribe stimulant laxative (senna) + softener (docusate) on opioid initiation. Treat: osmotic (macrogol/Movicol), stimulant (senna, bisacodyl, sodium picosulfate), prokinetic (prucalopride). Combination: stimulant + osmotic (best evidence). Refractory: methylnaltrexone SC (μ-opioid receptor antagonist, doesn't cross BBB — only peripheral side effects), naloxegol (oral), opioid rotation (less constipating = fentanyl, methadone). Bowel management plan: regular, proactive, rescue (enema, suppository, manual evacuation if impaction).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence50-90% advanced cancer
CauseOpioids (universal), immobility, low fibre, drugs
BristolType 1-2 (hard, separate)
ProphylacticLaxative for all opioid patients
First-lineStimulant (senna) + softener (docusate)
Second-lineMacrogol, lactulose
Third-lineRectal (suppository, enema)
MethylnaltrexonePeripheral opioid antagonist (subcut)
NaloxegolPeripheral opioid antagonist (oral)

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Faecal impactionOverflow, hard mass
Bowel obstructionDistension, vomiting, absent flatus
OpioidAll, no tolerance

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line for opioid-induced constipation? A: Senna + docusate (stimulant + softener), escalate to macrogol.
  1. 2. Q: Methylnaltrexone mechanism? A: Peripheral μ-opioid receptor antagonist; doesn't cross BBB, so doesn't reverse analgesia or cause withdrawal.
  1. 3. Q: Why prophylactic laxative on opioid? A: All opioids cause constipation, no tolerance, common, disabling.
  1. 4. Q: Most constipating opioid? A: Morphine (no advantage); less: fentanyl, methadone.
  1. 5. Q: Complications? A: Overflow diarrhoea, impaction, urinary retention, delirium, pseudo-obstruction, perforation.

topics.title: Cough Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Cough is a common, distressing symptom in palliative care, often in lung cancer, end-stage COPD, asthma, infection, post-nasal drip, ACE inhibitor, gastro-oesophageal reflux, or radiation pneumonitis. Assessment: dry vs productive, triggers, timing, character, associated symptoms. Investigation: clinical, CXR if indicated, sputum culture if infection. Management: treat reversible cause (infection, asthma, reflux, stop ACE, reduce secretions); demulcents (simple linctus, honey, glycerol); opioids (codeine 30-60mg q4-6h, morphine 2.5-5mg q4-6h IR for severe), low-dose morphine is most effective for cancer cough; antitussives (dextromethorphan, pholcodine); mucolytics if productive (carbocisteine); expectorants (guaifenesin); for cough-variant asthma, inhaled steroids. Non-pharmacological: humidified air, positioning, treating the cause.

Key clinical pearls:

  • Most common in lung cancer/COPD
  • Codeine 30-60mg q4-6h
  • Morphine 2.5-5mg q4-6h for severe
  • Treat reversible cause

Clinical Features and Presentation

Cough in palliative care is common, dry or productive, distressing, disrupts sleep. Causes: lung cancer, end-stage COPD, infection, post-nasal drip, ACE inhibitor, gastro-oesophageal reflux, radiation pneumonitis, pleural effusion, heart failure, lymphangitis.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical (dry vs productive, triggers, timing, character), CXR if indicated, sputum culture if infection, drug review (ACE inhibitor), reflux assessment.

Management

Treat reversible cause (infection, asthma, reflux, stop ACE, reduce secretions). Demulcents: simple linctus, honey, glycerol. Opioids: codeine 30-60mg q4-6h, morphine 2.5-5mg q4-6h IR (most effective for cancer cough). Antitussives: dextromethorphan, pholcodine. Mucolytics if productive (carbocisteine). For cough-variant asthma, inhaled steroids. Non-pharm: humidified air, positioning, treating the cause.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesTumour, infection, COPD, PE, ACEi
TreatmentTreat cause; antitussive
Simple linctusFirst-line
Codeine linctus30mg q4h
Morphine5-10mg q4h
MethadoneAlternative
OtherDemulcents, bronchodilators, antibiotics

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Lung cancerImaging, treat tumour
COPDBronchodilators, steroids
InfectionAntibiotics
RefluxPPI
Drug (ACE)Stop drug

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Most common cause in cancer? A: Lung cancer, end-stage COPD, infection.
  1. 2. Q: First-line for cancer cough? A: Codeine 30-60mg q4-6h; morphine 2.5-5mg q4-6h for severe.
  1. 3. Q: Treat reversible? A: Infection (ABx), asthma (inhaled steroid), reflux (PPI), ACE (stop), effusion (drain).
  1. 4. Q: Demulcent? A: Simple linctus, honey, glycerol (sugar syrup, soothing).
  1. 5. Q: Why not for productive cough? A: Suppresses sputum clearance; use mucolytic (carbocisteine) instead.

topics.title: Delirium In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Delirium in palliative care is a common (28-90%), serious, often missed complication. Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, altered consciousness, disorientation, hallucinations, agitation or hypoactivity. Reversible causes (40-50%): drugs (opioids, corticosteroids, anticholinergics), metabolic (hypercalcaemia, hyponatraemia, uraemia, liver failure), infection, dehydration, hypoxia, urinary retention, constipation. Terminal delirium (last days) often irreversible. Treatment: identify and treat reversible causes; non-pharmacological (reorientation, family, familiar objects); antipsychotics (haloperidol 0.5-2mg q4-8h, olanzapine 2.5-5mg nocte, levomepromazine 6.25-25mg), benzodiazepines (midazolam 2-5mg SC) for severe agitation/refractory.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Delirium is acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention, awareness, and cognition, with reduced ability to focus, sustain attention, or shift attention. Common in palliative care (up to 90% in last days/weeks), often under-recognised. Three subtypes: hyperactive (agitation, hallucinations — 25%), hypoactive (lethargy, withdrawal — 75%, missed), mixed. Causes: drugs (opioids, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, steroids), metabolic (Ca, Na, urea, glucose, liver failure), infection (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis), hypoxia, hypercapnia, dehydration, constipation, urinary retention, pain, brain (metastases, stroke). Terminal delirium: in last days, often irreversible.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Bedside assessment: 4AT, CAM (Confusion Assessment Method), time-of-day variation. Reversible causes: drugs, bloods (U&E, Ca, LFT, glucose, FBC, cultures), infection screen, O2/CO2, bladder, bowels, pain, brain imaging if focal signs. Avoid unnecessary investigations at end of life.

Management

Identify and treat reversible causes (drugs, metabolic, infection, pain, urinary retention, constipation). Non-pharm: reorientation, family presence, familiar environment, clocks, lighting, soft music, avoid restraints. Pharmacological: haloperidol 0.5-5mg SC/PO (first-line, for hyperactive or distressing), levomepromazine 12.5-50mg (second-line, broader, more sedating), olanzapine 2.5-5mg (alternative), benzodiazepines (only if alcohol withdrawal or seizures, midazolam SC prn or CSCI for refractory/agitated). Terminal delirium: address cause, family support, comfort care.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence28-90% palliative
FeaturesAcute, fluctuating, inattention, altered consciousness
Reversible causesDrugs, metabolic, infection, dehydration, hypoxia
TerminalLast days, often irreversible
Non-pharmReorientation, family, familiar objects
Haloperidol0.5-2mg q4-8h (first-line)
Olanzapine2.5-5mg nocte
Levomepromazine6.25-25mg
Midazolam2-5mg SC for severe agitation

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Delirium vs dementiaAcute, fluctuating, attention impaired
DepressionSlow onset, attention preserved
Drug effectBenzodiazepines, opioids

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Most common subtype in palliative care? A: Hypoactive (75%, often missed).
  1. 2. Q: First-line pharmacological? A: Haloperidol 0.5-5mg SC/PO.
  1. 3. Q: Reversible causes? A: Drugs (opioids, benzos, anticholinergics, steroids), metabolic (Ca, Na, urea, glucose, liver), infection, hypoxia, urinary retention, constipation, pain.
  1. 4. Q: Assessment tool? A: 4AT (≥4 = delirium) or CAM (acute onset, fluctuating, inattention, altered consciousness).
  1. 5. Q: Terminal delirium? A: In last days; often irreversible; family education, comfort care, sedation if distressed.

topics.title: Depression In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Depression in palliative care is common (5-50%) but underdiagnosed. Symptoms: persistent low mood, anhedonia, hopelessness, guilt, suicidal ideation, sleep/appetite changes, withdrawal. Distinguish from normal grief (which is reactive, transient, fluctuates). Screening: Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9). Treatment: psychological (CBT, supportive, dignity therapy, meaning-centred therapy); pharmacological (SSRIs — sertraline, citalopram, 4-6 weeks for effect; mirtazapine for sleep, appetite, anxiety; TCAs — amitriptyline — if neuropathic pain). Methylphenidate for rapid effect (2-4 days). For severe/refractory: ECT.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Depression in palliative care is common (15-50%), under-recognised. Persistent low mood, anhedonia (loss of pleasure), hopelessness, guilt, worthlessness, suicidal ideation, sleep/appetite changes, fatigue, psychomotor retardation. Differentiate from normal grief (which has waves), anticipatory grief, adjustment disorder. Risk factors: prior depression, advanced disease, poor pain control, social isolation, functional decline. Suicidal ideation is a key marker (not normal in grief).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Screening: PHQ-2 (Patient Health Questionnaire-2, ≥3), PHQ-9, HADS (Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale). Clinical interview. Risk assessment: suicidal ideation, intent, plan.

Management

Psychological: CBT, supportive psychotherapy, dignity therapy, mindfulness, meaning-centred therapy. Pharmacological: SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram — first-line, 4-6 weeks onset); mirtazapine (also appetite, sleep, weight gain); TCAs (amitriptyline, useful with neuropathic pain); psychostimulants (methylphenidate 5-10mg BD — rapid onset, end of life). Combination: psychological + pharmacological. ECT for severe/refractory. Social and spiritual support.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence5-50%
DifferentiateDepression vs normal grief
ScreeningHADS, PHQ-9
PsychologicalCBT, dignity, meaning-centred
SSRISertraline, citalopram (4-6 weeks)
MirtazapineSleep, appetite, anxiety
MethylphenidateRapid (2-4 days)
ECTSevere/refractory
GriefReactive, fluctuating, transient

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Normal griefWaves, can enjoy
Adjustment disorderWithin 3 months, less severe
Hypoactive deliriumAcute, fluctuating, attention impaired

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line antidepressant in palliative care? A: SSRI (sertraline, citalopram).
  1. 2. Q: How to differentiate depression from normal grief? A: Depression: persistent anhedonia, hopelessness, worthlessness, suicidal ideation; grief: waves, can enjoy moments, no suicidal ideation.
  1. 3. Q: When to use mirtazapine? A: Depression with insomnia, weight loss, poor appetite (sleep, weight gain side effects).
  1. 4. Q: Rapid-onset antidepressant? A: Methylphenidate (psychostimulant), 5-10mg BD, onset 1-2 days, useful at end of life.
  1. 5. Q: Suicidal ideation in pall care? A: Always assess; often related to uncontrolled pain, depression, existential distress; not normal.

topics.title: Dysphagia In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) is a common, distressing symptom in palliative care, often in head and neck cancer, oesophageal cancer, neurological disease (motor neurone disease, stroke, Parkinson's, dementia), and advanced debility. Causes: mechanical (tumour obstruction, stricture), neurological (bulbar palsy, neuromuscular), functional (weakness, cognitive, debility). Assessment: clinical, swallow assessment by speech and language therapist, video fluoroscopy if needed. Management: treat cause if possible (stent for oesophageal obstruction, radiotherapy, dilation), modify diet (soft, puréed, thickened fluids), feeding tubes (NG, PEG) — careful ethical consideration, mouth care, posture, calm environment. Consider advance care planning: eating for pleasure vs nutrition, comfort feeding, withdrawing artificial nutrition.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) in palliative care is distressing, common in head and neck cancer, oesophageal cancer, neurological disease (MND, stroke, Parkinson's, dementia), advanced debility. Causes: mechanical (tumour obstruction, stricture), neurological (bulbar palsy, neuromuscular), functional (weakness, cognitive, debility). Aspiration risk: pneumonia, choking.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical, SALT swallow assessment, video fluoroscopy if needed.

Management

Treat cause if possible (stent for oesophageal obstruction, radiotherapy, dilation), modify diet (soft, puréed, thickened fluids, IDDSI levels), feeding tubes (NG, PEG) — careful ethical consideration, mouth care, posture, calm environment. Consider advance care planning: eating for pleasure vs nutrition, comfort feeding, withdrawing artificial nutrition (ethically equivalent to withholding).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesHead/neck cancer, oesophageal, neurological (MND, stroke, Parkinson's, dementia), debility
SymptomsDifficulty swallowing solids/liquids, choking, coughing, weight loss, aspiration
AssessmentClinical, SALT swallow assessment, video fluoroscopy
ManagementTreat cause (stent, RT, dilation), modify diet, feeding tube (NG, PEG)
SALTSpeech and language therapist — swallow assessment, advice, exercises
DietSoft, puréed, thickened fluids; IDDSI levels
PEGPercutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy; consider ethics, advance care plan
Comfort feedingFor pleasure, not nutrition; quality of life
WithdrawalWithdrawing artificial nutrition ethically equivalent to withholding
AspirationRisk of pneumonia; treat; consider antibiotic if recurrent

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
OdynophagiaPainful swallow
GlobusSensation without dysphagia
Mechanical vs neurologicalSALT can differentiate

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line? A: SALT assessment + modify diet (soft, puréed, thickened fluids).
  1. 2. Q: Stent indication? A: Oesophageal obstruction (tumour, stricture); rapid relief, alternative to PEG.
  1. 3. Q: PEG considerations? A: Ethics (prolonging life vs quality), advance care plan, patient wishes, family.
  1. 4. Q: Withdrawing artificial nutrition? A: Ethically equivalent to withholding; comfort care at end of life; requires MDT and family discussion.
  1. 5. Q: Aspiration risk? A: Pneumonia, choking, choking on secretions; treat infection; comfort.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Dysphagia in pall care = SALT + modify diet + treat cause + ethics

topics.title: Hiccups Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Hiccups (singultus) are involuntary, intermittent spasms of the diaphragm, often self-limiting but in palliative care can be persistent (>48h) and distressing. Causes: gastric distension (reflux, overeating), metabolic (uraemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, hypokalaemia), CNS (stroke, tumour, infection), diaphragmatic irritation (tumour, pericarditis, pneumonia, abdominal distension), drugs (dexamethasone, benzodiazepines, opioids, dopamine agonists). Investigation: history, examination, bloods (U&E, calcium), CXR, abdominal imaging if indicated. Management: simple measures (sip water, paper bag rebreathing, Valsalva, swallow dry sugar, vagal stimulation), gabapentin (first-line for persistent, starting 100mg TDS titrated to 300-600mg TDS), baclofen 5-10mg TDS, metoclopramide 10mg TDS, haloperidol 1-3mg nocte, midazolam CSCI 10-30mg/24h for refractory, chlorpromazine, nifedipine; for intractable: anaesthetic block (phrenic nerve).

Key clinical pearls:

  • Gabapentin 100-300mg TDS first-line
  • Baclofen 5-10mg TDS
  • Treat underlying cause
  • Lasts >48h is chronic

Clinical Features and Presentation

Hiccups (singultus) are involuntary, intermittent spasms of the diaphragm, often self-limiting but in palliative care can be persistent (>48h) and distressing. Causes: gastric distension (reflux, overeating), metabolic (uraemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, hypokalaemia), CNS (stroke, tumour, infection), diaphragmatic irritation (tumour, pericarditis, pneumonia, abdominal distension), drugs (dexamethasone, benzodiazepines, opioids, dopamine agonists).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History, examination, bloods (U&E, calcium), CXR, abdominal imaging if indicated.

Management

Simple measures (sip water, paper bag rebreathing, Valsalva, swallow dry sugar, vagal stimulation), gabapentin 100mg TDS titrated (first-line for persistent), baclofen 5-10mg TDS, metoclopramide 10mg TDS, haloperidol 1-3mg nocte, midazolam CSCI 10-30mg/24h for refractory, chlorpromazine, nifedipine; for intractable: anaesthetic block (phrenic nerve).

  1. 5. Q: Lasts >48h is? A: Chronic hiccups (intractable if >1 month).

topics.title: Insomnia Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Insomnia is difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, common in palliative care due to pain, anxiety, depression, drugs (corticosteroids, opioids, antidepressants), symptoms (cough, breathlessness, pruritus, N&V), environment (noisy ward, light), existential distress. Management: identify and treat cause (pain, anxiety, depression, drugs, symptoms), sleep hygiene (regular schedule, dark/quiet, no screens, warm drink, no caffeine/alcohol late), relaxation techniques, cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I). Pharmacological: temazepam 7.5-15mg nocte (short-acting benzodiazepine, common first-line), zopiclone 3.75-7.5mg (Z-drug, alternative), promethazine 25-50mg (antihistamine, sedation), mirtazapine 15-30mg (antidepressant with sedation, useful if depression/appetite poor), melatonin 3-6mg (circadian rhythm). Avoid regular hypnotics in advanced disease (may cause confusion, falls).

Key clinical pearls:

  • Sleep hygiene + CBT-I
  • Temazepam 7.5-15mg
  • Treat cause (pain, anxiety, depression)
  • Avoid in advanced disease

Clinical Features and Presentation

Insomnia in palliative care affects 30-50%. Difficulty initiating, maintaining, or returning to sleep, with daytime consequences. Causes: pain, anxiety, depression, drugs (corticosteroids, opioids, stimulants, SSRIs), symptoms (cough, dyspnoea, pruritus, N&V, night sweats), environment (noisy ward, light), existential distress, frequent toileting (diuretic, polyuria). May be primary insomnia or secondary.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History (sleep pattern, onset, triggers, daytime function), pain, anxiety, depression, drugs, symptoms. Sleep hygiene assessment. Tools: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI).

Management

Treat cause: pain, anxiety, depression, drugs, symptoms, environmental (noisy, light). Non-pharm: sleep hygiene (regular schedule, dark, quiet, no screens, comfortable temperature, no caffeine/alcohol late, no long daytime naps), relaxation, mindfulness, CBT-I (gold standard in chronic insomnia). Pharmacological: temazepam 7.5-15mg nocte (short-acting, first-line, BZD), zopiclone 3.75-7.5mg (Z-drug), melatonin 3-6mg (circadian, elderly), mirtazapine 15-30mg (antidepressant, also useful for sleep, appetite, weight gain, anxiety), promethazine 25-50mg (antihistamine, less preferred in elderly due to anticholinergic). Avoid long-term hypnotics in advanced disease (confusion, falls).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence30-50%
CausesPain, anxiety, depression, drugs, environmental
TreatmentTreat cause; non-pharm; pharm
Sleep hygieneFirst-line
BenzodiazepineTemazepam, lorazepam
Z-drugZolpidem, zopiclone
Melatonin2-6mg
Amitriptyline10-25mg
Mirtazapine15-30mg (if depression)

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Pain-related insomniaTreat pain
Drug-induced (steroids)Dose early
AnxietyTreat anxiety
DepressionAntidepressant

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line hypnotic? A: Temazepam 7.5-15mg nocte (short-acting BZD).
  1. 2. Q: Best long-term treatment? A: CBT-I (gold standard).
  1. 3. Q: When to use mirtazapine? A: Insomnia with depression, weight loss, anxiety.
  1. 4. Q: Why avoid in elderly? A: Falls, confusion, anticholinergic side effects (antihistamines, prolonged BZD).
  1. 5. Q: Sleep hygiene? A: Regular schedule, dark/quiet, no screens, comfortable temp, no caffeine/alcohol, no long daytime naps.

topics.title: Malignant Bowel Obstruction Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Malignant bowel obstruction (MBO) is a common complication of advanced abdominal/pelvic malignancy (ovarian, colorectal, gastric). Symptoms: colicky abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting (may be faeculent), abdominal distension, constipation/obstipation. May be partial (some flatus) or complete. Conservative management: analgesia (opioid + anticholinergic hyoscine butylbromide for colic), antiemetics (cyclizine, haloperidol, levomepromazine), antisecretory (hyoscine butylbromide, octreotide to reduce secretions), decompress NG tube, IV fluids, corticosteroids (dexamethasone to reduce oedema). Surgery only in select patients (good performance, single obstruction site, expected survival >2 months).

Clinical Features and Presentation

MBO is mechanical or functional obstruction in advanced cancer (ovarian, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, small bowel). Causes: tumour, adhesions, paraneoplastic, opioid, anticholinergic. Symptoms: pain (colicky, then continuous), distension, vomiting (early in high, late in low), constipation, absent flatus.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical + AXR (dilated loops, air-fluid levels) + CT (site, level, cause).

Management

Surgery: consider if prognosis >2-3 months, single level, good PS. Medical: analgesia (opioid + anticholinergic hyoscine butylbromide), antiemetic (cyclizine, haloperidol, levomepromazine — avoid metoclopramide in mechanical), antisecretory (hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC, glycopyrronium 800mcg/24h, octreotide 250-500mcg/24h), dexamethasone 6-8mg IV, NG tube (vomiting), IV fluids (cautiously), stenting (colorectal, gastric outlet), venting G-tube.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseAdvanced abdominal/pelvic cancer (ovarian, colorectal, gastric)
SymptomsColic, N&V (faeculent), distension, constipation
Partial/completePartial has some flatus
ConservativeAnalgesia, antiemetic, antisecretory, NG, fluids, steroid
Hyoscine butylbromideColic + antisecretory
OctreotideReduce secretions
DexamethasoneReduce oedema
SurgerySelect: good PS, single site, survival >2m

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Mechanical vs functionalDrug-induced, paraneoplastic
Non-malignant obstructionAdhesion, hernia
ConstipationFaecal loading

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line medical? A: Analgesia (opioid + anticholinergic), antiemetic (cyclizine, haloperidol), antisecretory (hyoscine, glycopyrronium, octreotide), dexamethasone.
  1. 2. Q: Why avoid metoclopramide? A: Prokinetic; worsens colic, contraindicated in mechanical obstruction.
  1. 3. Q: Octreotide? A: Inoperable MBO; reduces secretions and pain.
  1. 4. Q: Surgery consideration? A: Single level, good PS, prognosis >2-3 months.
  1. 5. Q: Dexamethasone? A: Reduces bowel wall oedema, may relieve obstruction partially, antiemetic.

topics.title: Nausea And Vomiting In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Nausea and vomiting (N&V) in palliative care affects 30-60% of patients with advanced cancer. Pathophysiology: 4 main pathways (CTZ via 5-HT3/D2 in floor of 4th ventricle, vestibular apparatus via H1/M1, gut vagal afferents via 5-HT3, higher centres via cortex). Causes: metabolic (hypercalcaemia, uraemia, hyponatraemia), drugs (opioids, chemotherapy, antibiotics), mechanical (gastric stasis, bowel obstruction, raised ICP), vestibular, anxiety. Antiemetic choice by receptor/cause: haloperidol (D2 — chemical/CTZ), cyclizine (H1/M1 — vestibular/gut), ondansetron (5-HT3 — chemo/post-op/gut), metoclopramide (D2/5-HT4 — gastric stasis), levomepromazine (broad-spectrum), dexamethasone (raised ICP, multi-action), hyoscine (gut motility, secretions).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Nausea and vomiting (N&V) affects 30-60% of advanced cancer. 4 pathways: CTZ (5-HT3, D2, NK1), vestibular (H1, M1), gut vagal afferents (5-HT3, D2), higher centres (cortex, anxiety). Causes: metabolic (Ca, urea, Na), drugs (opioids, chemo, antibiotics, NSAIDs), GI (gastric stasis, obstruction, constipation), vestibular, raised ICP, anxiety. Antiemetic by receptor: haloperidol D2, cyclizine H1/M1, ondansetron 5-HT3, metoclopramide D2/5-HT4, levomepromazine broad, dexamethasone ICP, hyoscine gut motility.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History (timing, triggers, content, associations), examination (hydration, abdomen, fundoscopy for papilloedema), bloods (U&E, Ca, LFT, glucose), abdominal X-ray (obstruction), drug review, constipation check.

Management

Identify cause. Receptor-targeted antiemetic. First-line: haloperidol 0.5-1.5mg nocte (chemical/CTZ) or metoclopramide 10mg TDS (gastric stasis). Second-line: cyclizine 50mg TDS (vestibular/gut), ondansetron 8mg TDS (chemo/post-op/gut), levomepromazine 6.25mg TDS (broad-spectrum, last-line). Refractory: combination, levomepromazine, dexamethasone (4-8mg), CSCI, nasogastric decompression, palliative stenting.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence30-60% advanced cancer
PathwaysCTZ (5-HT3/D2), vestibular (H1/M1), gut (5-HT3), cortex
CausesMetabolic, drugs, mechanical, vestibular, anxiety
HaloperidolD2 — chemical/CTZ
CyclizineH1/M1 — vestibular/gut
Ondansetron5-HT3 — chemo, post-op, gut
MetoclopramideD2/5-HT4 — gastric stasis
LevomepromazineBroad-spectrum
DexamethasoneRaised ICP, multi-action
HyoscineGut motility, secretions

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Bowel obstructionDistension, absent flatus, X-ray
Raised ICPHeadache, papilloedema, CT
MetabolicHypercalcaemia, uraemia
DrugOpioid, chemo

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: 4 main pathways of N&V? A: CTZ (5-HT3, D2), vestibular (H1, M1), gut vagal afferents (5-HT3, D2), higher centres (cortex).
  1. 2. Q: Antiemetic receptor targets? A: Haloperidol D2; cyclizine H1/M1; ondansetron 5-HT3; metoclopramide D2/5-HT4; levomepromazine broad.
  1. 3. Q: First-line for opioid-induced N&V? A: Haloperidol (D2) or metoclopramide (D2/5-HT4).
  1. 4. Q: Refractory N&V in palliative care? A: Combination of above + levomepromazine 6.25mg, dexamethasone, CSCI.
  1. 5. Q: Bowel obstruction management? A: Cyclizine, haloperidol, dexamethasone, hyoscine (anticholinergic, antisecretory), octreotide; avoid metoclopramide (prokinetic, contraindicated).

Confusions and Mnemonics

topics.title: Pleural Effusions Symptomatic Management Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Pleural effusion in palliative care is accumulation of fluid in the pleural space, common in advanced cancer (lung, breast, lymphoma, mesothelioma, ovarian) and end-stage cardiac, renal or liver failure. Often recurrent on same side. Causes breathlessness, cough, chest discomfort. Diagnosis: clinical, CXR (blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus), confirmed by ultrasound (loculation, guided drainage). Management: symptomatic drainage (thoracentesis, up to 1-1.5L at a time to avoid re-expansion pulmonary oedema); indwelling pleural catheter (IPC, e.g., Rocket or PleurX) for recurrent malignant effusion (drains at home 2-3 times/week); pleurodesis (talc via chest drain or VATS) for malignant effusion (success 60-90%); treat underlying cause; opioids for dyspnoea; oxygen if hypoxic.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Pleural effusion in palliative care: accumulation of fluid in pleural space, common in advanced cancer (lung, breast, lymphoma, mesothelioma, ovarian) and end-stage cardiac, renal or liver failure. Often recurrent on same side. Causes breathlessness, cough, chest discomfort.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical, CXR (blunted costophrenic angle, meniscus), confirmed by ultrasound (loculation, guided drainage).

Management

Symptomatic drainage (thoracentesis, up to 1-1.5L at a time to avoid re-expansion pulmonary oedema); indwelling pleural catheter (IPC, e.g., Rocket or PleurX) for recurrent malignant effusion (drains at home 2-3 times/week); pleurodesis (talc via chest drain or VATS) for malignant effusion (success 60-90%); treat underlying cause; opioids for dyspnoea; oxygen if hypoxic.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesCancer (lung, breast, lymphoma, mesothelioma, ovarian), heart/renal/liver failure
SymptomsDyspnoea, cough, chest discomfort, fatigue
DiagnosisCXR, ultrasound (loculation, drainage guide)
ThoracentesisDrainage; max 1-1.5L at a time (re-expansion pulmonary oedema if more)
IPCIndwelling pleural catheter for recurrent malignant effusion (home drainage)
PleurodesisTalc (slurry or poudrage) for malignant; success 60-90%
VATSVideo-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for pleurodesis ± biopsy
Treat causeCancer (chemo/radiotherapy), heart failure (diuretic)
SupportiveOpioids for dyspnoea, oxygen if hypoxic
RecurrenceHigh in malignant (relieve with IPC or pleurodesis)

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Pleural thickeningMesothelioma, post-TB, imaging differentiates
Lung collapseImaging

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Max thoracentesis at a time? A: 1-1.5L (to avoid re-expansion pulmonary oedema).
  1. 2. Q: IPC for what? A: Recurrent malignant pleural effusion; home drainage 2-3x/week.
  1. 3. Q: Pleurodesis success? A: 60-90% with talc slurry or poudrage (VATS).
  1. 4. Q: Treat cause? A: Cancer (chemo/RT), heart failure (diuretic), liver/renal failure.
  1. 5. Q: When to avoid thoracentesis? A: Coagulopathy, small effusion, loculated.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Pleural effusion in pall care = Drain + IPC or pleurodesis; treat cause + dyspnoea

topics.title: Pruritus In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Pruritus (itch) in palliative care affects 10-30%. Causes: cholestasis (obstructive jaundice — common in pancreatic/liver cancer), uraemia, opioids (μ-receptor), paraneoplastic (lymphoma), xerosis, drugs, scabies, eczema. Treatment: identify and treat cause; cholestatic pruritus (cholestyramine 4g q6-12h, rifampicin 150-300mg daily, sertraline, naltrexone); uraemic pruritus (gabapentin, pregabalin, mirtazapine, UV-B phototherapy); opioid-induced (ondansetron 8mg, opioid rotation, naloxone); emollients, topical corticosteroids, antihistamines (limited benefit).

Clinical Features and Presentation

Pruritus (itch) affects 20-30% of palliative care. Causes: cholestasis (obstructive jaundice — pancreatic, cholangiocarcinoma, liver mets), uraemia (renal failure), opioid-induced (especially morphine; intrathecal morphine, fentanyl; less with methadone, hydromorphone), paraneoplastic (Hodgkin, leukaemia, solid tumours), dermatological (xerosis, scabies, drug eruption), iron deficiency, thyroid disease, psychogenic. Often distressing, disrupts sleep.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

History (timing, relationship to drugs, dialysis, jaundice), examination (skin — primary lesion, scratch marks, xerosis, jaundice, excoriations, papules), bloods (LFT, renal, Ca, ferritin, TFT, FBC), drug review. Skin biopsy if unclear.

Management

Treat cause: cholestasis (bile acid sequestrants — cholestyramine, colestipol, colesevelam; rifampicin; naltrexone; sertraline), uraemia (antihistamines, gabapentin, pregabalin, phototherapy), opioid (rotation — methadone, hydromorphone, fentanyl patch), dermatological (emollients, topical steroids, calcineurin inhibitors, menthol cream). Antihistamines: hydroxyzine 25mg, cetirizine, loratadine (non-sedating for daytime), chlorpheniramine. Other: gabapentin 300-900mg/day (uraemic, cholestatic, opioid), pregabalin, ondansetron (cholestatic), low-dose paroxetine or mirtazapine (cholestatic, paraneoplastic).

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesCholestasis, uraemia, opioids, paraneoplastic
CholestaticCholestyramine, rifampicin, sertraline, naltrexone
UraemicGabapentin, pregabalin, mirtazapine
Opioid-inducedOndansetron, rotation
EmollientsAlways
AntihistaminesLimited benefit

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
CholestasisJaundice, LFT
UraemiaCKD, RRT
OpioidTemporal, rotation helps
DermatologicalPrimary lesion
ParaneoplasticRefractory

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Common causes of pruritus in palliative care? A: Cholestasis, uraemia, opioid (especially morphine), paraneoplastic.
  1. 2. Q: First-line for cholestatic pruritus? A: Cholestyramine (bile acid sequestrant), then rifampicin, naltrexone, sertraline.
  1. 3. Q: First-line for opioid-induced pruritus? A: Opioid rotation (to methadone, hydromorphone, fentanyl); antihistamine; ondansetron.
  1. 4. Q: First-line for uraemic pruritus? A: Gabapentin 300-900mg/day; phototherapy; antihistamines.
  1. 5. Q: Why sedating antihistamines? A: Pruritus has both histamine and central (serotonergic) components; sedating H1 (chlorpheniramine, hydroxyzine) more effective.

topics.title: Respiratory Secretions Death Rattle Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Death rattle is noisy breathing from pooled respiratory secretions in the upper airways in dying patients (often in last 24-48h of life). Affects 12-92% of dying patients. Treatment: positional (side-lying), reassurance to family (sounds often worse than patient's experience), sublingual hyoscine hydrobromide (0.6mg q4-6h SC; or scopolamine patch 1mg/72h), glycopyrronium (0.2mg SC q6-8h), octreotide (subcut). Suctioning often ineffective and distressing. Prevention: avoid fluid overload in last days. Family distress is the main issue.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Death rattle is noisy breathing from pooled secretions in oropharynx and bronchi in dying patients (50% in last 3 days). Sounds distressing to family; patient usually unconscious. Mechanism: inability to clear secretions, muscle relaxation, accumulation. Once established, often difficult to clear.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical diagnosis, distinguish from pulmonary oedema (RHF, fluid overload) or infection (purulent secretions, fever).

Management

Prevention: adequate hydration (avoid excess IV fluids at end of life), positioning (lateral, semi-prone), suction (may not be effective if deep), family education. Pharmacological: anticholinergics to reduce secretion production: glycopyrronium 200-400mcg SC prn or 800-1200mcg/24h CSCI (first-line, no CNS), hyoscine butylbromide 20mg SC prn or 80-120mg/24h CSCI, hyoscine hydrobromide 0.4mg SC (crosses BBB, may sedate/confuse), atropine 1% eye drops SC/SL. Note: these reduce new secretions but don't clear existing.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
TypeNoisy breathing in dying
CausePooled secretions in upper airways
TimingLast 24-48h life
TreatmentPositioning, reassurance
Hyoscine hydrobromide0.6mg q4-6h SC
Glycopyrronium0.2mg SC q6-8h
OctreotideReduce secretions
SuctioningOften ineffective, distressing
FamilyAddress distress

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Pulmonary oedemaRHF, frothy secretions
PneumoniaFever, purulent, signs
BronchospasmWheeze, history asthma

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Best anticholinergic for death rattle? A: Glycopyrronium 200-400mcg SC (or CSCI 800-1200mcg/24h) — no CNS, peripheral only.
  1. 2. Q: Why not hyoscine hydrobromide? A: Crosses BBB, can cause sedation/confusion.
  1. 3. Q: Prevent with fluids? A: Avoid excess IV fluids in last days (worsens secretions).
  1. 4. Q: Suction? A: May help if secretions accessible; often ineffective if deep; avoid repeated suction (distressing).
  1. 5. Q: Family education? A: Sounds distressing but patient usually unconscious; reposition, gentle suction; medication reduces new secretions.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Terminal Agitation And Restlessness Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Non-Pain Symptoms in Palliative Care

Definition

Terminal agitation/restlessness is a common, distressing symptom in the last days of life, with motor agitation, confusion, moaning, calling out, picking at bedclothes. Causes: terminal delirium (most common), pain, urinary retention, constipation, hypoxia, hypercalcaemia, drug withdrawal, anxiety, spiritual distress, terminal hepatic/renal failure. Treatment: identify and treat reversible causes; non-pharmacological (reassurance, family, familiar environment, music, dim light); pharmacological (midazolam 2.5-5mg SC PRN, or continuous SC infusion 10-30mg/24h; haloperidol 1-2.5mg SC PRN; levomepromazine 6.25-12.5mg SC PRN; phenobarbital 100-200mg IM PRN if refractory). Consider palliative sedation if refractory.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Terminal agitation is restlessness, confusion, distress in last days of life. Causes: delirium (metabolic, drugs, infection, hypoxia), pain, urinary retention, constipation, drug withdrawal, dyspnoea, anxiety, existential distress, raised ICP, terminal organ failure. Reversible causes should be sought and treated. If refractory, palliative sedation may be considered.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical assessment, identify reversible causes (drugs, pain, retention, metabolic, hypoxia), bloods if appropriate (often not at end of life), family discussion.

Management

Identify and treat reversible. Non-pharm: calm environment, family presence, music, massage, low light, familiar items. Pharmacological: midazolam 2.5-5mg SC prn (first-line), CSCI 10-30mg/24h (refractory); haloperidol 1-3mg SC for delirium; levomepromazine 12.5-50mg SC/CSCI for refractory; opioids if pain. Palliative sedation for refractory: midazolam CSCI titrated, with consent, MDT, documentation, double-effect principle.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseTerminal delirium, pain, urinary retention, hypoxia
TreatmentTreat cause; non-pharm; pharm
Midazolam2.5-5mg SC PRN or 10-30mg/24h CSI
Haloperidol1-2.5mg SC PRN
Levomepromazine6.25-12.5mg SC PRN
Phenobarbital100-200mg IM for refractory
SedationPalliative if refractory

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Delirium (terminal)Hallucinations, fluctuating
PainVocalises, guards
Urinary retentionDistended bladder
DyspnoeaTachypnoeic, hypoxic

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line for terminal agitation? A: Midazolam 2.5-5mg SC prn; CSCI 10-30mg/24h for refractory.
  1. 2. Q: Reversible causes? A: Pain, urinary retention, constipation, drugs, metabolic, hypoxia, anxiety, dyspnoea.
  1. 3. Q: Palliative sedation principle? A: Midazolam CSCI for refractory symptoms; double effect; consent; MDT.
  1. 4. Q: Haloperidol vs midazolam? A: Haloperidol for delirium (psychosis, hallucinations); midazolam for anxiety, agitation, sedation.
  1. 5. Q: Levomepromazine? A: Antipsychotic + sedative + antiemetic; for refractory; broad-spectrum.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.title: Acute Dyspnoea In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Acute dyspnoea (breathlessness) is a common palliative care emergency in advanced cancer, end-stage cardiac/respiratory disease. Causes: tumour (pleural effusion, bronchial obstruction, lymphangitis, SVC), cardiac failure, infection (pneumonia, COPD exacerbation), anaemia, PE, anxiety, ascites. Management: opioids (oral morphine 2.5-5mg q4h or SC diamorphine 1-2mg prn or CSCI), benzodiazepines for anxiety (midazolam 5-10mg SC prn or CSCI, or diazepam 5-10mg PO/rectal), oxygen if hypoxic, positioning, airflow (open window, fan), positioning, treat reversible (infection, effusion drainage, blood transfusion, stenting). Non-pharmacological: breathing techniques, relaxation, family support.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Acute dyspnoea (breathlessness) is common in advanced cancer, end-stage cardiac/respiratory disease. Causes: tumour (pleural effusion, bronchial obstruction, lymphangitis, SVC), cardiac failure, infection (pneumonia, COPD exacerbation), anaemia, PE, anxiety, ascites, fluid overload.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Cause: clinical, CXR, oxygen saturation, BNP, bloods (FBC, U&E, ABG if indicated), ECG, sputum culture if infection.

Management

Treat reversible (infection, effusion drainage, anaemia, bronchospasm). Opioids (oral morphine 2.5-5mg q4h, SC diamorphine 1-2mg prn, CSCI for persistent). Benzodiazepines for anxiety (midazolam 5-10mg SC prn or CSCI, diazepam 5-10mg PO). Oxygen if hypoxic (SpO2 <88%, or symptomatic hypoxia). Positioning (sitting up, lean forward). Airflow (fan, open window, even if not hypoxic). Non-pharm: breathing techniques, relaxation, family support, calm environment.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesTumour (effusion, obstruction, lymphangitis), cardiac, infection, anaemia, PE, anxiety
OpioidsReduce perception of breathlessness; oral morphine 2.5-5mg q4h, SC 1-2mg prn
BenzodiazepinesAnxiety component; midazolam 5-10mg SC prn or CSCI
OxygenIf hypoxic (SpO2 <88%)
PositionSit upright, lean forward
AirflowFan, open window (sensory input, can be very effective)
ReversibleEffusion (drain), anaemia (transfuse), infection (ABx)
Non-pharmBreathing techniques, relaxation, family support
PrognosisDepends on cause; terminal breathlessness may respond to opioids

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
PulmonaryCancer, COPD, infection, PE, effusion, SVC
CardiacCCF, anaemia, PE
MetabolicAcidosis, anaemia
AnxietyOften comorbid

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line drug? A: Opioid (morphine 2.5-5mg q4h or SC diamorphine 1-2mg prn).
  1. 2. Q: Second-line/anxiety? A: Benzodiazepine (midazolam 5-10mg SC, diazepam 5-10mg PO).
  1. 3. Q: When oxygen? A: Hypoxic (SpO2 <88%) or symptomatic hypoxia; no evidence for non-hypoxic.
  1. 4. Q: Non-pharm? A: Positioning, fan, open window, breathing techniques, relaxation, family support.
  1. 5. Q: Treat reversible? A: Infection (ABx), effusion (drain), anaemia (transfuse), bronchospasm (salbutamol), PE (anticoagulation).

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Dyspnoea = Opioids + benzodiazepines + oxygen + airflow + position + treat cause

topics.title: Acute Pain Crisis In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Acute pain crisis (or pain emergency) is sudden, severe, uncontrolled pain in a palliative care patient requiring urgent intervention. Causes: pathological fracture, bone metastasis progression, spinal cord compression, biliary/ureteric colic, bowel obstruction, perforated viscus, infection, procedure-related. Management: rapid-onset strong opioid (SC morphine 5-10mg or IV/SC diamorphine, repeat at 30-60min, titrate), adjuvant (NSAID, dexamethasone for bone pain/colic, anticholinergic for colic), non-pharmacological (positioning, hot/cold, distraction), identify and treat cause, consider CSCI opioid if multiple doses, breakthrough plan.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Acute pain crisis is sudden, severe, uncontrolled pain requiring urgent intervention. Causes: pathological fracture, bone metastasis progression, spinal cord compression, biliary/ureteric colic, bowel obstruction, perforated viscus, infection, procedure-related.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Identify cause (history, examination, imaging). Pain assessment.

Management

Rapid-onset strong opioid (SC morphine 5-10mg or diamorphine 5-10mg, repeat at 30-60 min), adjuvant (NSAID, dexamethasone for bone pain/colic/MSCC, anticholinergic for colic, ketamine for refractory), non-pharm (positioning), identify and treat cause (RT for bone mets, stent for obstruction, surgery for fracture), CSCI, PCA for severe.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseFracture, metastasis progression, MSCC, colic, obstruction, perforation, infection
AssessmentSudden severe pain, often new character or site
TreatmentStrong opioid rapid onset (SC morphine 5-10mg or SC diamorphine 5-10mg)
RepeatRepeat at 30-60min, titrate to effect
AdjuvantNSAID, dexamethasone (bone pain, MSCC, cerebral oedema), anticholinergic (colic)
CauseIdentify and treat (radiotherapy for bone mets, stent for obstruction)
CSCIContinuous subcutaneous opioid infusion for sustained control
BreakthroughPlan for breakthrough doses (1/6 of total daily opioid)

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line? A: Rapid-onset strong opioid (SC morphine 5-10mg or diamorphine 5-10mg), repeat.
  1. 2. Q: Adjuvants? A: NSAID, dexamethasone, anticholinergic, ketamine.
  1. 3. Q: Treat cause? A: RT, stent, surgery, ABx.
  1. 4. Q: CSCI vs PCA? A: CSCI for sustained; PCA for severe uncontrolled.
  1. 5. Q: Dexamethasone indication? A: Bone pain, MSCC, raised ICP, colic.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Pain crisis = Rapid-onset opioid + adjuvant + treat cause + CSCI

topics.title: Catastrophic Haemorrhage In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Catastrophic haemorrhage is sudden, massive, often terminal bleeding in palliative care patients, requiring immediate emergency management. Causes: tumour erosion into major vessel (carotid blowout in head/neck cancer, aortic rupture, tumour friability — gastric, lung, cervical), coagulopathy (DIC, liver failure, anticoagulation), oesophageal varices (liver disease). Risk factors: prior bleeding, large necrotic tumour, proximity to major vessel, anticoagulation, low platelets, infection. Management: acute — call for help, sit up, apply direct pressure, IV fluids/cross-match, transfuse; consider reversing anticoagulation, tranexamic acid, platelets; control of bleeding (endoscopic/surgical/radiological if appropriate); deep sedation (midazolam + opioid IV) for catastrophic terminal bleed; anticipatory prescribing (midazolam 10mg + diamorphine 10mg, dark syringe, pre-drawn); support for family and staff.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Catastrophic haemorrhage is sudden, massive, often terminal bleeding in palliative care. Causes: tumour erosion into major vessel (carotid blowout in head/neck cancer, aortic rupture, tumour friability — gastric, lung, cervical), coagulopathy (DIC, liver failure, anticoagulation), oesophageal varices. Risk factors: prior bleeding, large necrotic tumour, proximity to major vessel, anticoagulation, low platelets, infection. May be predicted (warning) or sudden.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Clinical diagnosis, bloods (FBC, coagulation, cross-match), bloods for source (rarely useful in terminal event).

Management

Acute: call for help, sit up, apply direct pressure, IV fluids, cross-match, transfuse; consider reversing anticoagulation (vitamin K for warfarin, PCC for DOACs, protamine for heparin), tranexamic acid 1g IV/PO, platelets if thrombocytopenic. Control bleeding (endoscopic, surgical, radiological). Terminal bleed: deep sedation (midazolam 10mg + diamorphine 10mg IV, repeat) for catastrophic terminal bleed; family presence and support; staff debrief. Anticipatory prescribing: pre-drawn syringe (midazolam 10mg + diamorphine 10mg, dark syringe) in fridge; family education.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseTumour erosion, coagulopathy, varices, anticoagulation
SitesCarotid blowout (head/neck), aortic rupture, GI tumour, lung, cervical
AcuteSit up, pressure, IV fluids, cross-match, transfuse, reverse anticoagulation
Terminal bleedSedate (midazolam + opioid IV), stay with patient, support family
AnticipatoryPre-drawn syringe (midazolam 10mg + diamorphine 10mg) in dark syringe, in fridge
Tranexamic1g IV/PO, anti-fibrinolytic
ReversalVitamin K (warfarin), PCC (DOACs), protamine (heparin)
Carotid blowoutDirect pressure, surgical emergency if time; often terminal
Family supportCrucial; pre-brief on possibility; post-event debrief
StaffDebrief after catastrophic event

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First steps in catastrophic bleed? A: Call help, sit up, pressure, IV fluids, cross-match, transfuse.
  1. 2. Q: Anticipatory prescribing? A: Pre-drawn syringe (midazolam 10mg + diamorphine 10mg, dark syringe) in fridge for terminal bleed.
  1. 3. Q: Reversal of anticoagulation? A: Vitamin K (warfarin), PCC (DOACs), protamine (heparin), tranexamic acid.
  1. 4. Q: Carotid blowout? A: Direct pressure, surgical emergency if time; often terminal; family + staff support.
  1. 5. Q: Family support? A: Pre-brief on possibility; support during; debrief after; staff debrief.

topics.title: Hypercalcaemia Of Malignancy Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Hypercalcaemia of malignancy occurs in 10-20% of cancer patients, mediated by PTHrP (most common, humoral hypercalcaemia of malignancy), osteolytic metastases, or rarely 1,25(OH)2D production (lymphoma). Most common cancers: breast, lung (squamous), multiple myeloma, renal, head and neck, ovarian. Clinical features: stones, bones, groans, thrones, psychiatric overtones (polyuria, polydipsia, dehydration, bone pain, constipation, confusion). Calcium >3.5 mmol/L severe; >4.0 life-threatening. Treatment: IV normal saline (3-6L/day, forced diuresis), IV bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid 4mg over 15min or pamidronate 60-90mg over 2-4h, takes 2-4 days for full effect), calcitonin (rapid but short-lived effect, useful for severe), denosumab (in bisphosphonate-refractory or renal failure); loop diuretics only after rehydration; consider dialysis.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Hypercalcaemia of malignancy affects 10-20% of cancer patients. Mechanism: PTHrP (most — humoral, squamous lung, breast, renal, ovarian, thyroid), osteolytic metastases (breast, myeloma, lung), 1,25-(OH)2D production (lymphoma). Cancers: breast, lung, multiple myeloma, renal, head/neck, ovary. Symptoms: stones (renal, polyuria), bones (pain), groans (constipation, N&V, abdominal pain), thrones (polyuria), psychiatric overtones (confusion, depression, fatigue). Severe >3.5 mmol/L; life-threatening >4.0. ECG: short QT, prolonged PR, widened QRS.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Corrected Ca, albumin, PTH (suppressed), PTHrP (elevated), 25-OH vitamin D, 1,25-(OH)2D, LFT, ALP, U&E, ECG (short QT), phosphate (low in PTHrP). CXR, myeloma screen.

Management

IV normal saline 3-6L/day (corrects dehydration, increases calciuresis), IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4mg over 15min or pamidronate 60-90mg over 2-4h, takes 2-4 days for full effect), calcitonin 100-200IU SC q6-12h (rapid onset 4-6h, short-lived, useful for severe + bisphosphonate loading), denosumab 120mg SC (refractory or renal failure), loop diuretics only after rehydration, dialysis (severe/refractory/renal failure). Treat underlying cause.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
Prevalence10-20% of cancer patients
MechanismPTHrP (most), osteolytic, 1,25-(OH)2D (lymphoma)
CancersBreast, lung (SCC), multiple myeloma, renal, head/neck, ovary
SymptomsStones (renal), bones (pain), groans (constipation), thrones (polyuria), psychiatric overtones
SevereCalcium >3.5 mmol/L
InvestigationCorrected Ca, albumin, PTH (suppressed), PTHrP, ECG (short QT)
TreatmentIV NS 3-6L/day, IV bisphosphonate (zoledronate 4mg or pamidronate 60-90mg)
CalcitoninRapid onset (12h), short-lived, useful for severe + bisphosphonate
DenosumabAnti-RANKL, bisphosphonate-refractory or renal failure
Loop diureticsOnly after rehydration
PrognosisPoor (median survival 2-3 months)
DialysisSevere, refractory, or renal failure

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Primary hyperparathyroidismPTH high, not suppressed
Vitamin D toxicity25-OH vitamin D high
Milk-alkali syndromeAlkali ingestion
Granulomatous disease1,25-(OH)2D high (sarcoidosis, TB)

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line treatment? A: IV normal saline 3-6L/day, IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4mg or pamidronate 60-90mg).
  1. 2. Q: Mechanism? A: PTHrP (most), osteolytic metastases, 1,25-(OH)2D (lymphoma).
  1. 3. Q: Onset of bisphosphonate? A: 2-4 days; calcitonin rapid (4-6h) but short-lived.
  1. 4. Q: When to use denosumab? A: Bisphosphonate-refractory or renal failure.
  1. 5. Q: Symptoms mnemonic? A: Stones, bones, groans, thrones, psychiatric overtones.
