topics.title: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression Mscc Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC) is an oncological emergency with compression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina by tumour, leading to neurological deficit. Most commonly from vertebral metastases (prostate, breast, lung, kidney, myeloma, lymphoma). Clinical features: back pain (>95%, often first symptom, worse on movement, lying flat, Valsalva), sensory level, motor weakness, autonomic dysfunction (sphincter, bladder, bowel). Diagnosis: MRI whole spine (urgent, within 24h of clinical suspicion). Treatment: high-dose dexamethasone (10mg IV bolus then 4mg q6h), urgent neurosurgical/radiation oncology referral, definitive treatment (surgical decompression or radiotherapy, depending on suitability — Tokuhashi score, NOMS framework). Prognosis depends on pretreatment neurological status (ambulatory before = 80% remain ambulatory; paraplegic before = 5% recover).

Clinical Features and Presentation

MSCC is oncological emergency: compression of spinal cord/cauda equina by tumour causing neurological deficit. Causes: vertebral metastases (prostate, breast, lung, kidney, myeloma, lymphoma). Site: thoracic 60%, lumbar 30%, cervical 10%. Features: back pain (>95%, often first, worse on movement/Valsalva/lying flat), sensory level, motor weakness, autonomic dysfunction (sphincter, bladder/bowel — late, poor prognosis). Cord compression is medical emergency; pretreatment function = main prognostic factor (ambulatory 80% remain; paraplegic 5% recover).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

MRI whole spine URGENT (within 24h of suspicion) — gold standard. CT if MRI contraindicated. X-ray (late changes).

Management

High-dose dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg q6h PO/IV (immediately, before imaging if high suspicion). Urgent neurosurgical + radiation oncology referral. Definitive: surgical decompression (posterior ± anterior) or radiotherapy (RT alone for multi-level, radio-resistant tumour, no surgical option). Indications surgery: solitary, single level, good prognosis, mechanical instability, radio-resistant. Post-treatment: rehabilitation, pressure area care, bladder/bowel, psychological support.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseVertebral metastases (prostate, breast, lung, kidney, myeloma, lymphoma)
SiteThoracic (60%), lumbar (30%), cervical (10%)
Pain>95%, often first, worse on movement/Valsalva, local tenderness
MotorWeakness, paraplegia (late)
SensoryLevel, paraesthesia
AutonomicBladder/bowel, sphincter dysfunction (late, poor prognosis)
DiagnosisMRI whole spine URGENT (within 24h of clinical suspicion)
SteroidsDexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg q6h (start immediately)
TreatmentSurgical decompression (posterior ± anterior) or radiotherapy
Indications surgerySolitary spinal cord compression, good prognosis, mechanical instability, radio-resistant tumour, progression despite RT
RT aloneMulti-level disease, radio-sensitive tumour, no surgical option, palliative
PrognosisPretreatment ambulatory: 80% remain; paraplegic: 5% recover

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Cauda equinaLower motor signs, saddle anaesthesia, late bladder
Bone metastasis without compressionPain without neurology
Spinal stenosisOlder, degenerative, gradual

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: First-line management of suspected MSCC? A: High-dose dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg q6h, immediate; MRI whole spine urgent.
  1. 2. Q: Indications for surgery vs RT? A: Surgery: solitary, single level, good prognosis, mechanical instability, radio-resistant. RT: multi-level, radio-sensitive, palliative, no surgical option.
  1. 3. Q: Prognostic factor? A: Pretreatment neurological function (ambulatory 80% remain; paraplegic 5% recover).
  1. 4. Q: Most common site? A: Thoracic (60%), then lumbar (30%), cervical (10%).
  1. 5. Q: Most common cancers? A: Prostate, breast, lung, kidney, myeloma, lymphoma.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. MSCC = Back pain + neurology + MRI urgent; dexamethasone + RT/surgery

topics.title: Seizures In Palliative Care Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Seizures in palliative care patients can be due to primary brain tumour, brain metastases, metabolic disturbance (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, uraemia in renal failure), drug toxicity (tramadol, SSRIs, antipsychotics, ondansetron), stroke, hypoxia, infection, withdrawal (benzodiazepines, alcohol, antiepileptics). Management: acute (place on side, suction, oxygen, IV/SC midazolam 10mg or diazepam 10mg PR/IV, second-line phenobarbital or phenytoin loading), treat underlying cause, continuous midazolam infusion for status, maintenance antiepileptics (no loading required for therapeutic level). Prognosis: depends on cause; often terminal event in advanced cancer.

Clinical Features and Presentation

Seizures in palliative care can be due to primary brain tumour, brain metastases, metabolic disturbance (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, uraemia, renal failure), drug toxicity (tramadol, SSRIs, antipsychotics, ondansetron, antibiotics), stroke, hypoxia, infection (meningitis, encephalitis), withdrawal (benzodiazepines, alcohol, antiepileptics).

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

Bloods (glucose, U&E, Ca, Mg, LFT, drug levels), CT brain if indicated (new focal, head injury, status), EEG if non-convulsive status suspected.

Management

Acute: position (recovery, side), suction, oxygen, IV/SC midazolam 10mg (or diazepam 10mg PR/IV, buccal midazolam 10mg if no IV access), repeat if needed. Status epilepticus: continuous midazolam CSCI 5-10mg/24h or infusion; phenobarbital loading 50-100mg SC then 100-200mg/24h CSCI. Maintenance: antiepileptic already, or add (phenobarbital via CSCI convenient, levetiracetam 500mg BD). Withdrawal: restart/replace weaned drugs (benzodiazepines, opioids). Family education and support.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CausesBrain tumour, metastases, metabolic, drugs, withdrawal, stroke, hypoxia
AcutePosition (side), suction, oxygen, IV/SC midazolam 10mg or diazepam PR
StatusContinuous midazolam 5-10mg/24h SC infusion or midazolam SC prn
MaintenanceAntiepileptics already, or add (phenobarbital via CSCI convenient)
PhenobarbitalUseful via syringe driver, simple, 50-100mg SC loading, 100-200mg/24h CSCI
LevetiracetamAlternative, IV or oral, 500mg BD
Dying phaseDiazepam 10mg PR or buccal midazolam 10mg
WithdrawalRestart/replace weaned drugs (benzodiazepines, opioids)
ReassuranceImportant for family; recovery is often rapid

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
WithdrawalRestart drugs
Status (non-convulsive)EEG, may need treatment

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Acute management of seizure? A: Position (recovery), suction, oxygen, IV/SC midazolam 10mg (or diazepam PR, buccal midazolam).
  1. 2. Q: Status epilepticus management? A: Continuous midazolam CSCI 5-10mg/24h; phenobarbital loading 50-100mg SC then CSCI.
  1. 3. Q: Causes? A: Brain tumour, brain mets, metabolic (hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, uraemia), drugs, stroke, hypoxia, infection, withdrawal.
  1. 4. Q: Phenobarbital in dying? A: Via CSCI convenient, simple, controls seizures; sedation limit at end of life.
  1. 5. Q: Family education? A: Reassure, position, give rescue, call ambulance if not palliative context.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  1. Seizure = Position + midazolam + treat cause; phenobarbital in dying

topics.title: Superior Vena Cava Obstruction Svco Full
topics.html:
Related: [[../../../Pain and Palliative Care MOC]], [[../../../Davidson Chapter 8 - Pain and Palliative Care Hierarchy]]

[!info] Davidson alignment: Chapter 8: Pain and Palliative Care (24th Edition, Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Definition

Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) is a clinical syndrome from obstruction of blood flow through the SVC, most commonly due to malignancy (lung cancer 70-80% — small cell, squamous; lymphoma; breast; metastatic). Clinical features: facial/neck/arm swelling (especially morning), plethora (redness), distended neck and chest wall veins, dyspnoea, cough, orthopnoea, headache worse on stooping/bending, intracranial symptoms (confusion, seizures, visual). Onset: gradual (weeks). Subacute onset, no history of malignancy, no obvious cause → urgent imaging to identify cause. Diagnosis: CXR, CT with contrast (most sensitive), bronchoscopy/biopsy for histology. Treatment: general measures (elevate head, oxygen, steroids — dexamethasone 4mg q6h); definitive: chemotherapy (small cell lung cancer, lymphoma — rapidly responsive), radiotherapy; endovascular stenting (rapid, effective, palliative).

Clinical Features and Presentation

SVCO is clinical syndrome from obstruction of blood flow through SVC, most commonly malignancy (lung 70-80% — small cell, squamous; lymphoma; breast; metastatic). Features: facial/neck/arm swelling (worse morning, lying flat), plethora (redness), distended neck and chest wall veins, dyspnoea, cough, orthopnoea, headache worse on stooping, intracranial symptoms (confusion, seizures, visual). Subacute onset (weeks), no history of malignancy → urgent imaging to identify cause.

Investigation and Management Summary

Investigations

CXR (widened mediastinum, mass), CT chest with contrast (most sensitive — obstruction site, cause, collateral vessels); tissue biopsy (bronchoscopy, mediastinoscopy, image-guided).

Management

General: elevate head, oxygen, dexamethasone 4mg q6h. Definitive treatment of cause: SCLC/lymphoma: chemotherapy (rapid response); radiotherapy. Endovascular SVC stenting: rapid palliative relief (24-48h) for severe SVCO or when other options limited.

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CategoryKey Points
CauseMalignancy 70-80% (small cell lung, squamous, lymphoma, breast, thyroid)
SymptomsFacial/arm swelling, plethora, distended veins, dyspnoea, orthopnoea, headache
Worse onBending forward, lying flat, coughing (raises venous pressure)
DiagnosisCXR, CT chest with contrast; tissue biopsy (bronchoscopy, mediastinoscopy)
TreatmentElevate head, oxygen, dexamethasone 4mg q6h, treat cause
DefinitiveSCLC/lymphoma: chemotherapy (rapid response); radiotherapy; endovascular stent (rapid palliative)
StentEndovascular SVC stenting for rapid relief (24-48h)
PrognosisDepends on cause; SCLC better than NSCLC

Differential Diagnosis

DifferentialDistinguishing Feature
Heart failure (facial oedema)JVP, hepatomegaly, treatment differs
Pericardial effusionTamponade, echocardiogram
Facial vein thrombosisDifferent distribution

Viva Questions

  1. 1. Q: Most common cause? A: Malignancy 70-80% (small cell lung, squamous, lymphoma, breast).
  1. 2. Q: Diagnosis? A: CXR + CT chest with contrast; tissue biopsy for histology.
  1. 3. Q: Rapid palliative relief? A: Endovascular SVC stenting (24-48h).
  1. 4. Q: First-line for SCLC/lymphoma? A: Chemotherapy (rapid response).
  1. 5. Q: General measures? A: Elevate head, oxygen, dexamethasone 4mg q6h.

Confusions and Mnemonics

  • See clinical features

topics.html:
Davidson 24e Ch 8 (Colvin, Fallon) — Palliative Care Emergencies

Overview

Malignant spinal cord compression (MSCC), superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO), hypercalcaemia of malignancy, catastrophic haemorrhage, seizures, acute pain crisis, acute dyspnoea.
